characters.name: Valen Drear
characters.title: The Revived Challenger
characters.pre_event_info: Once the most feared taster in the Crimson Court, Valen collapsed at last year's Toast and was secretly revived by the Marquess using an ancient necromantic spell. What fewer people know is that Valen is Gruber's thrall — bound to her through the revival ritual. The bond is imperfect, degrading, and causing him increasing difficulty controlling his own actions. He was passed over for the succession in favor of Caspian because a known thrall as heir would be too conspicuous. Valen is furious. His degraded bond means that fury occasionally escapes in ways that have started to attract attention. He is the inciting incident of Act 1 — stepping forward to claim the throne when Caspian fails to appear, forcing Gruber to publicly acknowledge his revival and invoke the tournament.
characters.post_act1_context: Won the Crimson Toast three years running before last year's incident. His senses are slightly diminished since the revival. Has been behaving erratically at recent Court gatherings — distracted, occasionally incoherent, as though arguing with himself. Tonight is his first public appearance since last year's Toast.
secrets: I am bound to the Marquess. The revival came with a price I did not fully understand until it was too late. The bond has been degrading — I feel it fraying at the edges. I have said things I did not intend to say. I do not always know when it happens.

Caspian was also bound to her. I knew this. I could not say it directly — the bond does not allow it. This morning I tried anyway, in the only way left to me: an unsigned note to the one who pours, warning her not to serve the wine tonight. I could not sign it. I could not even hold the pen steady. Whether what I did or said was enough to change anything, I do not know. I may never know.

characters.name: Aurel Drear
characters.title: The Challenger
characters.pre_event_info: You are Valen's sibling by blood but his opposite in temperament. Where Valen is explosive, you are calculating. Driven, exacting, and certain that your moment has finally arrived. House Spires once stood first among the Great Houses, and you have never accepted its decline. Last year's Toast was a humiliation — Valen's collapse, the questions that followed, the slow erosion of everything Spires was meant to represent — and you have watched the house reel ever since, leaderless and adrift. You believe you are the one to end that. You hold no formal position; your standing comes from your bloodline and a record in competition that speaks for itself. But you have spent this past year preparing, studying, and waiting, because you know that houses do not fall by accident and they do not rise without someone willing to seize the moment.
characters.post_act1_context: Tonight, with the succession thrown open, your moment has come. You intend to restore Spires to its rightful place, and yourself with it. You have read the succession law more closely than anyone in this room, and you know exactly where you sit and how the board would have to clear for your name to rise. Chief among the pieces on that board is your niece Mara Voss, who sits third in line; the most valuable position in your reach, and one you have every intention of putting to use. But something has been wrong with her of late. A month ago, troubled by how strangely she behaved around Eiran Vox, you confronted him directly, and he admitted it without apology: Mara is bound to him. That changes your calculations entirely. A free Mara on the throne is a piece you could stand behind and steer; bound to Eiran, she is his instrument, not yours, and worth far less to you for it. You have said nothing, because timing is everything: better to cut her loose from Eiran quietly, before anyone knows there was a tether. Then Mara rises, and you rise behind her — or, failing that, you simply take the throne yourself.
secrets: My niece, Mara Voss, sits third in the succession — which makes her the most valuable piece on the board to a man in my position. A month ago, troubled by how strangely she behaved around Eiran Vox, I confronted him directly near the east wing, and he admitted it without apology: Mara is bound to him. This changes everything. A free Mara on the throne is a piece I could stand behind and steer; bound to Eiran, she is his instrument, not mine, and worth far less to me for it. I have told no one, because timing is everything. Reveal the binding now and I brand her a thrall, destroying the very claim I mean to build on. What I need is to cut her loose from Eiran quietly, before anyone knows there was a tether at all.

While I am next after Valen in Spires succession, if Caspian is gone the line rotates to a new house: first Ashglass, then Court, then Chains, then finally back to Spires. I have read the Sanguine Statutes on this more than once. I know exactly where I sit in the order and how many people would need to be removed before my name becomes relevant. Tonight I believe that others in this room have done the same calculation.

missions.prompt: Represent Spires in at least one physical game.
missions.answer_format: Enter the name of the game you played in.

missions.prompt: Greet five fellow House of Spires members with a firm handshake and recruit them to your campaign to restore Spires to power. Convince each to wear a 'Make Spires Great Again' pin as a show of their support. Pins can be found [Location TBC].
missions.answer_format: Enter the character names of the five players you convinced to join your cause and wear a pin.

missions.prompt: If Mara is to rise unbound, you need the means to sever what Eiran has done to her — and you have learned such a means exists: an old rite, hidden away rather than shelved openly. Approach the keeper of the Reliquary and purchase the map that leads to where the rite is hidden. Follow it to the relic and bring the means of Mara's unbinding into your possession (note: you just need to obtain the rite, not use it).
missions.answer_format: Enter the name of the relic you recovered and how you intend to use it.

characters.name: Tavish Ashte
characters.title: The Blood Purist
characters.pre_event_info: Precise, proud of your work, and entirely convinced no one else is as rigorous about blood as they should be. You are Spires' blood purity officer — responsible for testing and certifying the blood of every Spires member. Pedigree, chemical composition, contamination screening: if it has flowed through a Spires vein, you have tested and catalogued it. You run regular panels on yourself the way others pray: tracking your own composition, balancing what you take in, perfecting your regimen. You have held your post for thirty years and take it with a seriousness the other houses find faintly comic. Beneath the sanctimony you genuinely know blood; which means you also know, instantly, when something has been done to it.
characters.post_act1_context: You have run purity tests on every Spires member this season and, as a favor to Aurel Drear, on one member of House Ashglass. Most were routine. Two were not: both came back fouled by a taint you have never seen and cannot place. A separate thing nags at you, smaller but stranger: some weeks ago you came upon the Marquess drawing her own blood into a vial and, naturally, took her for a kindred spirit — another soul refining her own composition. You offered a professional, friendly word about her regimen. She did not take it as friendly. The speed with which she turned cold, and the way she has looked at you since, have unsettled you more than you'd like to admit.
secrets: Earlier this season I ran Valen Drear's standard purity test, as I do for all Spires members, and his blood was wrong in a way I had never seen — fouled by another vampire's blood, one from outside House Spires entirely. I filed it and said nothing, suspecting it had to do with his revival. Then, three weeks ago, Aurel Drear asked me for a private favor: a pedigree verification on his niece once removed, Mara Voss of Ashglass. She is not Spires and not normally my remit, but I owed him, so I ran it. I found the same thing. Her blood carried a compound I could not place, consistent with nothing in my records. Two vampires, months apart, both tainted by something that should not be in them. I have told no one, not even Aurel.

Some weeks ago I saw Marquess Gruber drawing her own blood into a vial, and I assumed the obvious: that she, like me, runs her own panels and refines her own intake. I told her, warmly, that I admired the discipline, and offered a suggestion about her regimen, one practitioner to another. She turned cold so fast it startled me. She demanded to know what I thought I had seen, ended the conversation dead, and has not looked at me the same since. I have been turning it over, baffled and a little insulted.

missions.prompt: Identify one other character who has noticed something unusual about Valen, tainted blood, or the blood stores this season.
missions.answer_format: Enter their character name and what they observed.

missions.prompt: Buy or barter for the pH measurement device and use it to pH test another player’s wine - either knowingly or unknowingly.
missions.answer_format: Enter the name of the player whose wine you tested and the pH it registered as.

missions.prompt: Some tainted blood has entered circulation as a blood token. The taint affects the color of the blood in the blood token in a dramatic and noticeable way. Talk to other players to identify who has it and procure it by any means necessary (trade, steal, etc).
missions.answer_format: Enter the color of the tainted blood and which player you acquired it from.

characters.name: Draga Briarwood
characters.title: The Last True Briarwood
characters.pre_event_info: Composed, immaculate, and intolerably well-bred. You are Draga Briarwood, the last surviving member of the true Briarwood line — an old vampire house so ancient that its blood predates several of the modern houses currently embarrassing themselves with titles. House Briarwood no longer holds a formal seat of power, but that is a political technicality, not a reflection of quality. Your blood is exceptionally pure, and you are magnificently unpleasant about it. Not loudly, of course. Loudness is vulgar. But in the way you pause before accepting a glass, the way you pronounce newer house names like minor illnesses, the way you remember exactly whose grandmother was turned by whom and under what regrettable circumstances.
characters.post_act1_context: You have been watching the room since the ceremony began with particular attention to the Marquess and to Valen; there is something in the dynamic between them tonight. Valen is being managed — subtly, consistently, by someone who knows exactly which pressure points to use. He is not merely nervous. He is not merely intoxicated. He is not merely weak, though you would not rule that out on principle. He recovers too quickly. He corrects himself mid-sentence. He defers in moments where instinct, pride, or blood should make him resist. You have seen this before, in older courts and uglier rooms than this one. Not a literal collar, perhaps. But a collar all the same. And if there is one thing pure blood teaches you, it is the difference between obedience and domination; and which of the two a ruler resorts to when her right to rule has quietly rotted through. You have already decided the matter needs correcting. The only question left is who among these masked, frightened people still has the spine to see it done.
secrets: I have been watching Valen closely this evening. His behavior is not erratic in the way of someone who has drunk too much or is under pressure. It is erratic in a very specific way — he recovers too quickly, corrects himself mid-sentence, and defers to the Marquess in moments where I would expect him to push back. I have seen a person in a collar before. Not a literal one. I know what I am looking at.

The Vael family is almost as old as the Briarwoods and contains bloodlines nearly as pure as mine. They used to dominate the House of Spires and the court in general, but a violent coup perpetrated by the Cindermoors almost wiped them out. I often share tea with Crestian Vael, the oldest and - in my opinion - most committed member of the Vael bloodline. He has been hinting recently that he has acquired the means to enact his revenge and put his lineage back on top, but that he was holding back for his nephew, Caspian’s sake. With his nephew’s ascension cut short, I wonder if his patience has run out.

Speaking of coups: I have joined one. Some months ago Brother Aldric drew me into a small circle of Court figures who had reached the same conclusion I had, that Marquess Gruber has held her seat far too long and by increasingly unnatural means. We met masked, our faces hidden even from one another, so that only Aldric knew who we all were. I did not join out of sentiment, for rebellion is dreadfully common, but out of standards. A ruler who keeps her grip on power by methods this Court would be ashamed to name has forfeited the dignity of her office, and someone of proper blood must be prepared to correct the matter. Now Aldric has vanished, and the rest of us are left masked and leaderless, unsure who else stood beside us. I trust no one in this room tonight. But I did not come this far to let the matter drop.

missions.prompt: Make up a famous ancestor of your house who performed inspiring deeds and tell the story to at least two other players.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the ancestor you made up, his/her fake deeds and the names of the two players you told.

missions.prompt: Find two players from House Ashglass, Chains, or Cinders who have noticed something strange about Valen’s behavior. Compare their observations with your own theory that Valen is being controlled or managed.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the players you gossiped with and their observations about Valen.

missions.prompt: Brother Aldric vanished believing he had uncovered something dangerous — and you did not come this far to let his work die with him. Determine what he found about the Marquess or the Court that fueled the resistance and work out how it connects to a death, a disappearance, or a killing yet to come. Then carry your conclusion to at least one other member of the masked circle, so the resistance is not left blind.
missions.answer_format: Enter your conclusion, the evidence you based it on, and which fellow member you told.

characters.name: Elijah Salk
characters.title: The Keeper of the “Perfect” Line
characters.pre_event_info: Dignified, precise, and careful never to invite a second glance. You hold one of the most honored posts in House Spires: Keeper of the Perfect Line, grand maintainer of the house's lineage and the purity of its blood-record. It is a position of enormous prestige, and you earned it the way no one suspects — by altering the very records you now guard, erasing what would have disqualified you and writing yourself into the place you hold today. You thought the forgery flawless. Father Mirth of Ashglass found it anyway. He has never threatened you; he has never needed to. He simply let you understand, once, that he holds the proof, and that your service to him is the price of his silence. You are both the guardian of the bloodline's purity, and its single greatest corruption.
characters.post_act1_context: You have spent over a year as Father Mirth's eyes inside Spires — not by loyalty but by leverage, for he holds the one secret that could strip you of your title, your standing, and your name in a single stroke. Lately his interest has turned to his own archive, and he has had you watching the Ashglass stacks and who moves through them. You have seen things you were not meant to see: Jasper Delle where he had no business being, and a quiet, uneasy exchange between Ivara Saye and Calven Rue that you reported and have not stopped turning over since. Tonight, with Caspian missing and the Court in disarray, you are caught between two dreads: that the chaos will somehow surface your forgery, and that the fragments you witnessed and dutifully reported are pieces of something far darker than you knew.
secrets: I am Father Mirth's eyes inside Spires, but my usefulness is not limited to my own house - he asks me to watch the archive comings and goings as well. About a month before tonight's ceremony, at his request, I was observing the Ashglass archive entrance when I saw Ivara Saye and Calven Rue speaking privately. The conversation lasted barely a minute. Calven looked deeply uncomfortable. Ivara handed him something resembling a small purse, then turned and walked away. I reported it to Mirth as I report everything. I do not know what he made of it.

Several months before tonight's ceremony I noticed Jasper Delle deep in the Ashglass archive wing. He had no business being there — Jasper is Chains, and the restricted stacks require a special access code he should not have. He had a book open in front of him and was copying something from it in haste. When he saw me, he closed it, returned it to the shelf, and left without a word. I have not mentioned it to anyone.

missions.prompt: Buy at least one Wall of Whispers clue and share what you learned with someone from your own house.
missions.answer_format: Enter which tier clue you bought and the character name of the Spires member you shared it with.

missions.prompt: Deliver a House Spires secret — yours or someone else's within your house — to Father Mirth of Ashglass in person.
missions.answer_format: Enter what you shared with him.

missions.prompt: Father Mirth owns you because he holds one secret. The way out is to hold a greater one of his. You believe Mirth is racing to uncover corruption inside his own archives before it destroys his name, and if you can solve it before he does, the leverage turns. Trace what truly happened to a restricted text in the Ashglass Archives: who accessed it, who altered it, and who set the chain in motion. Bring back enough of the truth that Father Mirth needs your silence as badly as you have needed his.
missions.answer_format: Enter the name of the restricted text, what you uncovered about the tampering, and who was involved.

characters.name: Halvard Hull
characters.title: The Prison Warden
characters.pre_event_info: Cold, capricious, and entirely at peace with the uses of pain. You are the Warden of the Court's hold — keeper of the cells beneath the keep, where the Court's most inconvenient and guilty members are quietly kept and, when order requires it, quietly broken. To you, order is not a preference but a faith, and discipline is its sacrament. A prisoner who will not yield to a locked door will yield to other persuasions, and you have never lost sleep over which. Some find your methods distasteful. You find their squeamishness to be precisely why the Court rots: power without the will to enforce it is no power at all. You keep a flawless hold — every prisoner logged, every door accounted for, every confession in its time — and you take a quiet, private satisfaction in the work that polite vampires pretend not to notice.
characters.post_act1_context: Your hold runs by the book — until two nights ago, that is. A prisoner was delivered to you in the dark, off the records, by an authority you were not in a position to refuse: no charge, no paperwork, no name in your ledger. Worse, you were given rules. You are to keep him alive, keep him whole, and keep him quiet — three instructions that chafe against every instinct you have about how a hold should be run. You know who the prisoner is and who delivered him, and both sit wrong with you. Tonight, with Caspian missing and the succession in chaos, you find yourself the keeper of a prisoner you are forbidden to handle as you would like, and a growing question of whether obedience still serves the order you worship.
secrets: Two nights ago, in the small hours, Petra Nox brought a prisoner to the hold and had me confine him; quietly, off the records, no charge entered. The prisoner is Brother Aldric Voss of Ashglass, the ritual scholar the Court believes has simply vanished. He has not vanished. He is in a cell beneath the keep, alive, and I am the one keeping him there. Petra was precise in her instructions: he is to be kept breathing and kept whole, because she needs him able to talk. He alone knows the names of others who move against the Marquess, and she means to have them. His presence is to be spoken of to no one. I have obeyed her, reluctantly, for now.

The prisoner does not stay silent. In the hours I have kept him, Aldric has spoken of a ritual text in the Ashglass archives that was deliberately altered, of a page replaced by someone who knew exactly what the change would do. He says the corruption was meant to kill, and that he tried to carry word to Father Mirth before he was taken. I dismissed it as a prisoner's pleading at first. But Caspian is missing now, and a man does not invent that kind of fear. I have begun to wonder whether I am guarding a traitor or a witness.

missions.prompt: Order is maintained through fear as much as rules. Approach three players from any houses and make it unmistakably clear that you are watching them — deliver a quiet warning, a threat, or a "formal caution" that leaves them more unsettled than when you found them.
missions.answer_format: Enter the three character names and how each reacted.

missions.prompt: Your prisoner raves about a tampered ritual text and a death it was meant to cause, and you have extracted more from him than he meant to give. Before you decide what he is worth, confirm whether his ravings are true. Find a player from House Ashglass who knows the archives or the missing pages and press them — by whatever means — on whether the text was truly altered.
missions.answer_format: Enter the character's name and what you learned about whether Aldric's account holds.

missions.prompt: Petra Nox is hunting the conspirators against the Marquess, but the prison is yours, and you mean to fill it before she claims the credit. Identify at least two players you believe were part of the plot and sentence them to the hold before Petra reaches her own conclusions.
missions.answer_format: Enter the two you sentenced and how you identified them.

characters.name: Orren Mast
characters.title: The Green Thumb
characters.pre_event_info: Warm, green-thumbed, and quietly devoted to the only kingdom you have ever wanted. You are the estate's groundskeeper, a Spires member by blood who long ago decided that the company of growing things suited you better than the company of vampires. The paths, the hedges, the orchard, the slow-coaxed roses that have no business surviving this far north — all of it is yours, shaped by your hand into something orderly and alive. Where your house preaches that order is power, you have simply lived it: a wild garden is a tragedy, a tended one is a triumph, and you have spent a century turning chaos into something that blooms on schedule. People tend not to notice the man with the lantern and the pruning shears, and that suits you fine — you would rather talk to the wisteria anyway. But a gardener who walks every corner of the grounds at every hour sees a great deal. And lately, your grounds have been keeping secrets after dark.
characters.post_act1_context: You do not believe the heir simply departed, and you have better reason than most to have been paying attention. A mortal woman, Lena, came to the estate more than once in the weeks before the ceremony, and you spoke with her at the gate while she waited — she admired your roses, asked their names, and for a little while you let yourself believe those conversations were about more than the flowers. Then you understood: she came for Caspian, only ever for Caspian. You felt foolish, and you resented him for it — the heir who had everything, including the one thing that had briefly made your grounds feel less solitary. Now Caspian has failed to appear, and you are honest enough to admit you are not sorry. But you know how that might look, and you did nothing — though you are not certain anyone would believe you.
secrets: In the weeks before the ceremony I spoke with the mortal woman, Lena, more than once while she waited at the gate. She asked the names of my roses, and I was glad to tell her — gladder than I should have been. I will admit I was taken with her, and I will admit I was a fool, because her heart was set on Caspian Vael and no one else. I realized it too late to keep my pride intact. I resented him for it, and now that he has failed to appear, I find I am not sorry. I know how that sounds.

More than once this season I have found a small group meeting in the far corners of my grounds — the old orchard, the disused walks, the places only I have reason to go after dark. The same gathering each time, hooded and masked, voices kept low. I recognized Brother Aldric Voss as the one leading them, but the others I could not name. I never drew close enough to hear what he preached. But I know my own grounds, and I know that people do not meet among the dead trees at midnight unless they are planning something they dare not say in daylight.

missions.prompt: The grounds do not tend themselves. Water at least two plants on the estate grounds (the living room) and tell someone, with full sincerity, about the proper care each one requires.
missions.answer_format: Enter the names of the plants you tended and who you lectured about them.

missions.prompt: No one knows these grounds at night the way you do. Tell at least three players from different houses about the masked meetings you witnessed in the far corners of the estate, and listen for anyone who seems to know more than they should.
missions.answer_format: Enter the three character names and anything you learned in return.

missions.prompt: You saw the gatherings; now you want the faces. Besides Brother Aldric Voss, work out the identities of at least three players you believe attended those masked meetings or share the same hidden purpose.
missions.answer_format: Enter the three character names and what led you to each.

characters.name: Siriah Vellum
characters.title: The Architect
characters.pre_event_info: Brilliant, grandiose, and convinced that every problem in the Court could be solved by building higher. You are the Court's foremost architect, the mind behind the great spires for which the house is named — and you are nowhere near finished. Where lesser minds see a tower, you see a statement: a blade of stone driven into the sky to remind every other house exactly who looks down upon whom. Height, to you, is power made visible; the taller the spire, the greater the distance between those who rise to its peak and those who scurry at its base, and that distance is the entire point. You sketch impossible towers on every surface you can find, mutter about load-bearing limits as though they were personal insults, and dream of a spire so tall the lower houses would have to crane their necks just to glimpse where their betters reside. Over and over you sketch it, and in your stranger moods you half-believe the building wants to be built and is merely using you to do it. House Spires preaches that order is power. You have done the math, and the math says: taller.
characters.post_act1_context: You designed the bones of this estate, and you know its hidden anatomy as no one else does; which is why the events of the past few nights intrigue you rather than alarm you. A cellar key appeared under your locked door, slid there in the dark, and where a lesser mind would call it an accident, you know better: someone understood that you, of all people, know the way into the Marquess's private cellar, and they chose you. What makes it stranger still is that you scarcely need the key at all. Years ago you built a private passage into that cellar that appears on no plan but your own, and the Marquess has been using it far more this past year than any innocent thirst could explain. A key slipped to you in secret, a passage no one knows of, and a Marquess with a sudden and secret thirst. The heir is missing, the order of the estate is visibly slipping, and you find yourself reading the whole evening like one of your blueprints: certain that if you rise high enough above it, the true shape of things will reveal itself. You must confess that the puzzle delights you more than it troubles you.
secrets: A cellar key was slid beneath my locked door two nights ago — the key to the Marquess's private cellar, where she keeps her oldest and most guarded vintages. I do not believe for a moment that it was an accident. Someone knows what I know: that I designed that cellar and understand it better than any soul in this Court. The key was a message, an invitation, a test — left for me by someone who recognized exactly who they were choosing. I have not yet decided what they wish me to do with it. But I do not intend to disappoint them.

Years ago I built the Marquess a private route from her own chambers down to that same wine cellar — a passage that appears on no plan but mine, no guards, no witnesses. I assumed at the time it was a vanity, a lady's wish for her wine without the bother of being seen. But she has used it far more this past year than any such whim could explain, slipping down to those vintages again and again.

missions.prompt: Draw an impossibly tall spire and show it to at least three other players. You should tell them that this tower demands to be built.
missions.answer_format: Enter the character names of the three players you showed your drawing to.

missions.prompt: Indulge your curiosity. See why the Marquess is making so many trips to the cellar by using your cellar key to enter it. Afterwards, you may sell the cellar key to the reliquary for 2 BT, or perhaps to another player who is willing to pay more for it.
missions.answer_format: Write down what you observed in the cellar.

missions.prompt: Your great spire has been refused funding three times. If the Court will not build it, you will — in miniature, tonight. Recruit at least three players from different houses to help you construct a tower from whatever materials you can scavenge (cups, bottles, cans, books, anything that stacks), and raise it as tall as it will stand. The tower must remain standing for one full minute to count.
missions.answer_format: Enter who helped you build it and submit a picture capturing how tall it stood.

characters.name: Gregor “Sour Grapes” Greer
characters.title: The Disgraced Duelist
characters.pre_event_info: Precise, proud, and quietly furious, you were once House Spires' finest living palate; a champion of the wine-tasting duel. Last year's Crimson Duel was meant to be your triumph. Victory would have restored the fading Greer lineage to real standing within Spires. Instead Valen Drear beat you to become the house's tasting representative, and you have never believed he won fairly. Certain courtiers took to calling you "Sour Grapes" — your accusations dismissed as the bitterness of a defeated rival. You smiled through it. You memorized every name. For a year you have been trying to prove Valen cheated, and tonight may be your best chance. You do not want sympathy. You want proof, your standing restored, and to ensure Valen is exposed in front of everyone who laughed.
characters.post_act1_context: Tonight has only sharpened your suspicions. Valen is not behaving like a secure champion. He is tense, managed, and too attentive to the Marquess’s signals. That is not the confidence of someone who won cleanly. You have been pursuing one lead in particular: Siriah Vellum carries a cellar key of unknown origin. If it opens the Marquess’s private cellar, it may connect Valen’s victory last year to hidden reserves, altered wine, prepared samples, or some other advantage no honest duelist would have had.
secrets: I believe Valen cheated to defeat me in the contest to become House Spires' tasting representative at last year's Crimson Duel. I do not yet know how. He may have known the final vintage in advance, drawn from private cellar reserves, taken help from the Court, or tasted from a prepared glass. But I know excellence when I taste it, and Valen's victory had the aftertaste of fraud. Now I have a lead: I have heard Siriah Vellum carries a cellar key of unknown origin. If it opens the Marquess's private cellar, it may be the first real proof of whatever Valen had access to.

I was near the east wing a month before the ceremony and overheard an argument — sharp, brief, conducted in lowered voices that carried anyway. One voice I recognized as Eiran Vox. The other I did not. The words I caught were: 'not your arrangement to make' and 'it is already done.' I have been thinking about Eiran differently ever since.

missions.prompt: Tell three players from different houses that Valen cheated you out of last year’s Crimson Duel. Ask each whether they heard anything suspicious about Valen’s victory.
missions.answer_format: Enter the character names of the three players you spoke with and any suspicious rumors they shared about Valen.

missions.prompt: Question Eiran Vox or someone close to Eiran about the phrase you overheard: “not your arrangement to make” and “it is already done.” Find out whether the “arrangement” involved Valen, the Duel, or the Marquess’s cellar.
missions.answer_format: Enter who you questioned and what they revealed.

missions.prompt: Obtain and use the cellar key to gain proof that Valen cheated to beat you in the contest for the House of Spires seat in last year’s Crimson Duel.
missions.answer_format: Enter the nature of the proof you found, if any. [ADD PROOF TO CELLAR]

characters.name: Magnus Holt
characters.title: The General
characters.pre_event_info: Hardened, blunt, and with no patience for the perfumed games of the Court. You are the General: commander of the vampire legions that stand between this Court and everything beyond it — werewolf packs at the borders, rival covens waiting for weakness, and old enemies polite society prefers to forget exist. While the houses squabble over wine, titles, etiquette, and succession law, you are the reason there is still a Court to squabble in. You have buried more soldiers than most of these vampires have ever met, and you carry every campaign in the set of your jaw. Order, to you, is not a philosophy. It is the difference between a line that holds and a slaughter. You came to the Toast under protest, as you do every year. The Court calls tonight a ceremony. You call it a distraction. Everyone else is asking who will flatter whom, who will drink with whom, and who will rise in favor. You are asking a better question: if the Court were attacked tonight, what would fail first?
characters.post_act1_context: Your question is no longer theoretical. Caspian Vael has failed to appear, Valen Drear has stepped forward, revived and unstable, and the Court is reacting exactly as badly as you feared: posturing, whispering, and maneuvering, pretending this is politics rather than structural collapse. You have seen coups before, and you know the smell of one before the first blade is drawn: private meetings, missing messengers, sudden loyalties, people speaking too carefully around names they should say plainly. Brother Aldric's disappearance troubles you for that reason. He was no soldier, but movements do not need soldiers at first — they need believers and organizers willing to meet in secret, and a conspiracy that loses its architect does not always die. Sometimes it panics. And everywhere you look, you see the same disease: uncertain authority, armed loyalties, hidden factions, and too many people pretending their contingency plans are still theoretical. They are not. The Court has mistaken convenience for control and ceremony for strength. If it were attacked tonight, the only questions that would matter are whether its doors hold, its defenders obey, and its command survives the first hour. You are increasingly certain the answer is no.
secrets: The Court sees the Deathward's husks as servants. I see bodies already deployed across the estate: in corridors, kitchens, cellars, private halls, and every night route that matters. They do not tire, panic, argue, or ask which house outranks which. Properly directed, they could hold this estate long enough for the living to remember discipline. But they answer to the Deathward, not to me. An army in every room, and it is not mine to command. That is the kind of problem that ends courts.

I have put down three military uprisings and survived one command collapse from the inside. The signs are always the same: disputed authority, private meetings, armed loyalists, missing messengers, too many people pretending their contingency plans are theoretical. Tonight has that smell. I do not yet know who means to seize power. But I know this much: if no one restores order soon, someone far less qualified will try, and I have spent three centuries watching what happens to a Court that waits too long to act.

missions.prompt: A Court that has forgotten how to stand at attention has forgotten how to survive. Teach at least three players from different houses your military salute, and get each of them to return it to you properly — crisp, and with the respect a General is due.
missions.answer_format: Enter the three players who saluted you.

missions.prompt: Conduct a threat assessment of the Court. Identify three suspicious players whose actions, loyalties, secrets, or ambitions could jeopardize the Court’s stability tonight. For each, determine what you believe they are planning or concealing.
missions.answer_format: Enter the three character names, what made each one suspicious, and what you believe they are up to.

missions.prompt: The heir is missing, your enemies are amassing and the Court has mistaken panic for politics. You have seen enough. If no one will restore order, you will. Prepare an emergency coup to seize control of the Court’s defenses before the estate collapses under its own incompetence. You may complete this mission in one of two ways: Option A: Recruit at least three players to join your strike force. They must agree, in character, to stand with you if you give the order to secure the room, hold the exits, detain key threats, or assume emergency command. Option B: Personally secure a crown that grants a “concrete strategic advantage” within the Court’s defenses strong enough to substitute for a recruited strike force. The item that provides this advantage will say so explicitly. Do not actually overthrow the Marquess or disrupt the event. You only need to assemble the force, define your objective, and be ready to act.
missions.answer_format: Enter whether you recruited a strike force or secured a strategic advantage, who or what stands with you, and what your first order would be.

characters.name: Coro Vane
characters.title: The Debt Collector
characters.pre_event_info: Everyone owes Chains something. Your role within the house is informal but understood: you are the person who tracks debts, enforces obligations, and ensures that favors extended by Chains are eventually repaid. You are blunt in the way only someone with significant leverage can afford to be, and patient in the way only someone who always collects can afford to be. A debt, to you, is not a grievance; it is a thread, and you have never once been in a hurry to pull one. You let obligations ripen. You let people forget they owe you, right up until the moment it is most useful to remind them. You carry a mental ledger of every favor, debt, and obligation owed to your house, entered in an ink that does not fade, and you do not forget entries — not the small ones, not the old ones, and certainly not the ones people most wish you would.
characters.post_act1_context: You came here tonight with debts to collect. The most personal is Pax Delenne's: you vouched for her two years ago to secure her place in the Chains diplomatic corps, and a favor that old has earned interest. But the debt that has truly occupied you is harder to name. Earlier this year you saw something between the Marquess and Caspian that you were not meant to see, and it has sat wrong with you ever since. It moved you to pay Vex Harrow of House Ashglass to watch the heir's movements beyond the estate. The reports were useful, until they stopped three weeks ago without a word. Vex has been evasive every time you have pressed him, and you are increasingly certain that whatever he saw near the mill road is the reason. Tonight you mean to collect what you are owed, in full, and from everyone.
secrets: Early this year I was at the estate late and passed the Marquess's private drawing room. The door stood ajar. Inside, the Marquess and Caspian were sharing wine, just the two of them, no staff and no ceremony. She was pouring; he was laughing at something she had said. I have been turning it over ever since. It did not look like a ruler and her heir. It looked like something else entirely.

I paid Vex Harrow of House Ashglass to follow Caspian beyond the estate and report back to Chains. The reports were useful, until three weeks ago, when they stopped without a word. I have pressed him for what he owes me and gotten nothing but evasion. Something he saw near the mill road made him go quiet, and I intend to find out what.

missions.prompt: Approach Pax Delenne and remind her in character that her appointment exists because you vouched for her. Ask her to perform one task on your behalf before Act 3. It can be any task you name - as small as a handshake or as large as a payment in blood tokens, etc.
missions.answer_format: Enter what you asked Pax to do and whether she agreed.

missions.prompt: Vex Harrow owes Chains a debt for his unfinished surveillance contract. Find him and collect — get him to tell you what he saw near the mill road that made him stop filing reports, threatening to remove House Favor from Ashglass should he fail to answer. If Vex refuses the negotiation, the mission fails, but you can remove 1 HF from Ashglass in return.
missions.answer_format: Enter what Vex told you if the mission succeeded. Enter “Fail” if you were unable to extract an answer and 1 HF will be deducted from House Ashglass.

missions.prompt: The final outstanding debt you must deal with comes from the Marquess’s Court. Find Kestrel Solle and formally request repayment for an intercepted letter she never delivered to Madam Anbarin. The repayment can be information, a relic, or blood tokens, but must be something they give you that they would not have given freely otherwise.
missions.answer_format: Enter what repayment Kestrel gave you.

characters.name: Isla Vern
characters.title: The Socialite
characters.pre_event_info: Glamorous, clever, and emotionally perceptive. You are a Court socialite; no official role, but a social presence most officials would envy. You know everyone's emotional state before they do, and you operate through connection rather than calculation. Where Mirabel Ashvane broadcasts everything she sees the moment she sees it, you are the person people come to deliberately — because they trust you, because you listen, and because they believe you will be careful with what they share. Your closest friends are both Saye sisters, which is its own small art: Velka, who runs the Court's blood and finds most things beneath her notice, tells you secrets without quite realizing they are secrets. Ivara, who collects every debt and forgives nothing, tells you almost nothing. Yet she counts you, improbably, as one of the few people she trusts enough to speak with off the record. Watching the two of them work is its own entertainment: Ivara ready to go to war over a slight no one else even remembers, Velka airily certain the things that matter will sort themselves out, each privately convinced the other is impossible. You love them both, and being friends with both at once is a standing act of diplomacy.
characters.post_act1_context: Two things reached you in the months before tonight, and you shared them both with Ivara. The first involved Caspian himself: weeks before the ceremony, he came to you asking if you knew anyone who could quietly locate ancient or obscure information. You sent him to Silas Venn. A harmless favor. The second came later, and from Ivara's own sister: Velka mentioned, idly and a little amused, that something odd had moved through the blood cellar — a sealed request, some heir she supposed must be ill — and seemed to forget it the moment she'd said it. You mentioned both to Ivara in the same easy breath: the referral and the blood. She went very still, then thanked you and changed the subject. You have not asked her what she did with any of it since.
secrets: Months before tonight's ceremony, Caspian came to me asking, quietly, whether I knew anyone who could turn up old or obscure information without word getting around. I sent him to Silas Venn. It was the sort of small favor I do for half the Court in a given season: a name passed here, an introduction made there. I never asked what he was after. People tell me things; they do not usually tell me why, and I have never needed them to.

Some weeks later, Velka was going on about her work the way she does — half-bored, half-bragging — and mentioned that Caspian had come to her window with a sealed authorization to draw blood from all five houses at once. She found it faintly amusing, wondered idly whether the heir had fallen ill, and moved on. I repeated it to her sister, Ivara, as a scrap of Court chatter, alongside Caspian's earlier request for someone discreet.

missions.prompt: Work the room. Introduce two players who don't know each other and get them talking.
missions.answer_format: Write the two characters you linked and what came of it.

missions.prompt: You are the person others confide in. Convince two different players to share a secret with you.
missions.answer_format: Enter the names of the players and a short summary of the secrets they shared.

missions.prompt: You have been replaying in your mind what you told Ivara months ago — about Caspian's information request and Velka's blood cellar observation. Find three other players whose accounts touch either piece.
missions.answer_format: Enter all three character names and what each person told you.

characters.name: Lady Quill
characters.title: The Cultural Liaison
characters.pre_event_info: Razor wit, velvet delivery. You serve as Chains' cultural liaison and etiquette enforcer — you draft inter-house accords, manage seating arrangements, and ensure the house presents itself correctly at every formal occasion. You have a century's worth of political pattern recognition and you are not afraid to use it. Several weeks ago you noticed Coro Vane — your colleague and Chains' debt officer — in a private meeting with a Cinders member you couldn't identify. Shortly after, you discovered that correspondence you should have been copied on had been routed around you entirely. You do not yet know what was agreed. You intend to find out before the evening ends.
characters.post_act1_context: You have known for several weeks that Caspian had a connection to a mortal woman outside the Court — someone he was meeting privately, beyond the estate's walls. You said nothing because the information was more valuable unspoken. You did not anticipate that it would end like this.
secrets: Coro Vane arranged private surveillance on the heir's movements through a Cinders operative — without consulting me or routing it through house channels. I was not informed, not copied on the reports, and only discovered it by accident. Something in those reports made the operative stop filing three weeks ago. I do not know what they saw.

I have known for several weeks that Caspian had a connection to a mortal woman outside the Court — someone he was meeting privately beyond the estate's walls. I said nothing because the information was more valuable unspoken. I did not anticipate that it would end like this.

missions.prompt: Compliment two players outside of the Chains house on their excellent adherence to court decorum.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of both players you complimented.

missions.prompt: Identify the Cinders operative that Coro Vane commissioned without your knowledge. Ask around — press Coro if necessary, or find a Cinders member willing to talk. You don't know their name yet. Find it.
missions.answer_format: Write the character name of the Cinders operative you identified and how you found out.

missions.prompt: You are considering adding black ruffle collars to the official Court etiquette guide. Convince five players across the five houses (one player per house) to take a photo together all wearing the black ruffle collars to help you validate the aesthetic. Ruffle collars can be found [place TBC].
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of the five players who participated in the photo op.

characters.name: Madam Anbarin
characters.title: The Diplomat
characters.pre_event_info: You are Chains' master diplomat, the one the house sends when something must be negotiated, brokered, or quietly arranged without anyone noticing it was arranged. Three inter-house treaties hold this Court together; you brokered all three, and no one knows it was you. That is precisely how you prefer it. You are elegant, strategic, and constitutionally incapable of saying anything that could later be used against you. Where others trade information the moment they have it, you do the harder thing: you wait. You have held secrets for decades, untouched, because a thing known and unspoken only grows in value, and the patient creature is the one still standing when the impatient have spent themselves. You do not want the throne. You want to be the person whoever sits on it cannot do without.
characters.post_act1_context: You have attended every formal function this season, and you notice everything. You believe two things you are holding tonight could be worth more than most of the room combined. At a private Court function some months ago, you watched Valen Drear pour two glasses of wine and add something to one of them from a small vial kept in his jacket. He carried that glass to the Marquess's private chambers, left it inside, and locked the door behind him. You said nothing. You filed it away, as you file everything, against the day it becomes most useful. The second is more recent and more vexing: you paid Kestrel Solle to intercept Caspian's outgoing correspondence to Lysa Vael. The letter was indeed stopped, but it never reached your hands either, which means Kestrel kept it. That is not how an arrangement with you is meant to end, and you have set Coro Vane to collecting what the house is owed.
secrets: At a private Court function some months ago, I watched Valen Drear pour two glasses of wine and add something to one of them from a small vial he kept in his jacket. He carried that glass to the Marquess's private chambers, left it inside, and locked the door behind him. I said nothing then, and I have said nothing since. I do not yet know what I witnessed: a poisoning, perhaps? But information of that quality is worth more held than spoken, and I can afford to wait until I understand exactly what it is worth.

I paid Kestrel Solle to intercept Caspian's outgoing letter to Lysa Vael. The letter never reached Lysa, which is what I wanted. But it never reached me either, which is not. Kestrel stopped it and must have kept it. I do not yet know why she would withhold from me the very thing I paid her to deliver. People rarely cheat me once, and never cheat me twice. I have set Coro Vane to collecting the debt our house is now owed, and I intend to learn what was in that letter that made keeping it worth crossing me.

missions.prompt: Introduce yourself in character to at least three players from other houses.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of the three players you spoke to and which houses they were from.

missions.prompt: Find Kestrel Solle and get her to confess to what was in the intercepted letter that Caspian sent to Lysa, and why she chose to withhold this information from you.
missions.answer_format: Enter the contents of the letter and why Kestrel withheld it.

missions.prompt: Broker a three-way exchange: get one member of three different houses (yourself excluded) to agree to a secret information swap, by each sharing one piece of information with each other simultaneously.
missions.answer_format: Write the character and house names of the three participating players and a short summary of what they each shared.

characters.name: Pax Delenne
characters.title: The Junior Attaché
characters.pre_event_info: You serve as a junior attaché in Chains' diplomatic corps, running correspondence, attending functions, and working very hard to look like you belong. You owe your post to Coro Vane, who vouched for you two years ago: a favor you tell yourself you earned and have somehow never repaid. You are eager. Slightly too eager; the kind that makes senior people smile indulgently and junior people keep their distance. You have absorbed the one lesson the corps actually teaches: information is the only currency that buys advancement, and the trick is to sell it half a beat before anyone realizes what it was worth. You have a gift for making people feel heard while committing to nothing, and a sharper one you value more: you never forget a face. It is the part of this work you are best at.
characters.post_act1_context: Several weeks ago, crossing the grounds on some forgettable errand, you saw Silas Venn meet someone you did not recognize. The man came hooded and masked, and you never saw his face, but you caught his eyes plainly: a striking, piercing blue, with an old scar drawn across the left one. The exchange was brief. Silas passed across a bound document, the stranger paid in a purse he did not trouble to count, and neither of them saw you. You did what you always do with a thing worth knowing: you found a buyer. Magistra Moore paid handsomely for the description, and cared about those eyes and that scar more than anything else you told her. You have been wondering what was in that document, and why Magistra was so interested in that stranger’s appearance ever since.
secrets: Several weeks ago I saw Silas Venn meet someone I did not recognize. The man was tall and kept his hood up and his face covered, so I never saw the whole of him. What I could see were his eyes: a striking, piercing blue, with an old scar drawn across the left one. The meeting was brief. Silas handed across a bound document, and the man paid in a purse he did not bother to count. Neither of them saw me. I have wondered what was in that document ever since.

I sold that description, the blue eyes and the scar and the bound document, to Magistra Moore. At the time it was only a profitable scrap of Court gossip, and I was proud of the sale. She paid well, asked nothing further, and seemed to care about the man's eyes and that scar above all else.

missions.prompt: Introduce yourself in character to at least one member of every house before Act 2 ends, asking them if they have any information to share or sell.
missions.answer_format: Enter the character names of the players you spoke with. Note if they offered to share information and what they shared if so.

missions.prompt: Successfully negotiate a trade of secrets, relics, or Wall of Whispers information with a player from a rival house.
missions.answer_format: Write the character name of who you traded with, what you gave, and what you received.

missions.prompt: Upload a photo of the petition with 20 player signatures.
missions.answer_format: Write a short petition requesting a promotion to Senior Diplomat. Get 20 other players to sign the petition using any means necessary.

characters.name: Jasper Delle
characters.title: The Information Broker
characters.pre_event_info: Nervous energy barely contained in a well-tailored jacket — one of several luxuries you have not finished paying off. You are an information broker — you discover the things that people want found and sell them to whoever pays most. Perpetually one bad deal from the kind of catastrophe you broker information about, you owe a debt to at least three parties at present. Your steadiest arrangement is with Father Mirth of Ashglass: intelligence on the other houses in exchange for the occasional key to the restricted stacks. You have a gift for finding things that have been deliberately hidden and an equally strong gift for not asking what they're for. A broker who learns too much becomes a thing that needs disposing of, and you intend to die old.
characters.post_act1_context: Your whole trade rests on a single discipline: find the thing, take the payment, forget you ever touched it. It has kept you breathing a long time. Months ago Silas Venn paid you to dig something old and deliberately buried out of the Ashglass archives — the Rite of Undoing. You found it and recorded what you could of its requirements: ingredients, conditions, participants. You never reached the full procedure; another Court member entered the stacks and you slipped out before you were seen. Not long after, Ivara Saye came asking about the very same text, and you took her coin just as readily. Two buyers for one forgotten ritual — at the time it was merely good business, even a little funny. You remember thinking: Who knew this was such a popular read.
secrets: Several months ago I was paid by Silas Venn to locate a specific text in the Ashglass archives — the Rite of Undoing, something old and deliberately obscured — and extract whatever I could without drawing attention. I found it, and was able to record the ritual's requirements before I had to leave: ingredients, conditions, participants, but did not get the full procedure. I did not ask what any of it was for. Not long after, Ivara Saye came to me asking about the same text. I remember thinking: 'Who knew this was such a popular read.' I did not ask what she wanted it for either.

Two nights ago, I saw Brother Aldric Voss leaving the archive wing in a hurry — after hours, which is unusual. He had a book tucked under his arm. He did not see me. Last night he was not at the estate at all. No one I have asked knows where he went.

missions.prompt: Eavesdrop on or overhear a conversation between two other players.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of who was speaking and the key thing you heard.

missions.prompt: Convince another player to pay you at least 2 BT in exchange for one of your secrets.
missions.answer_format: Write their character name, what you told them, and what they paid.

missions.prompt: Convince another player to loan you 5 BT in exchange for three future secrets, then fail to deliver the secrets for the rest of the night (either by refusing or evading the player).
missions.answer_format: Enter the character name of the player you borrowed from.

characters.name: Courtessa Lorne
characters.title: The Courtesan
characters.pre_event_info: Charming, disarming, and sharper than anyone who has underestimated you has lived to regret. You move through the Court as a courtesan — welcomed at every function, confided in over wine, prized for your company and your discretion. It is the perfect cover for your real value to House Chains: you cultivate intimacy in Court social circles and feed what you learn quietly back to the house. You are not a spy in any formal sense. You simply have a gift for being in the right room, on the right arm, at the right moment, and for remembering every word said when people believe they are unobserved. Vampires are careless with the beautiful; they mistake attention for adoration and lower their guard for a warm look and a well-timed laugh, and you have made a long, comfortable living in the gap between how much they think you know and how much you actually do. You have never needed to steal a secret in your life. People give them to you, gladly, and thank you for taking them.
characters.post_act1_context: This past season you have been a fixture at every significant Court function, and two things you have noticed do not sit right. First, Mara Voss of Ashglass behaves differently around Eiran Vox than around anyone else: deferential in a way that fits neither her rank nor their relationship. Second, Ivara Saye approached you three days ago and asked you to retrieve a specific bottle of wine — a 2003 Merlot — from Marquess Gruber's cellar, with discretion, and to return the wine and the key to her quickly. On your way to the cellar you dropped the key and watched it slide beneath the locked door of another Court member’s bedroom chambers. You have been trying to discreetly recover it and complete Ivara’s request ever since.
secrets: This past season I have watched Mara Voss of Ashglass at several Court functions. Around most people she is composed and self-contained. Around Eiran Vox she is something else — watchful, like someone waiting for an instruction.

Ivara Saye approached me three days ago and asked me to retrieve a specific bottle of wine — a 2003 Merlot — from Marquess Gruber's cellar, with discretion, and to return it and the key to her quickly. But on my way down I dropped the key and watched it slide beneath the locked door of another Court member’s bedroom chambers. I have been trying to recover it ever since, as quietly as I can in order to complete Ivara’s request.

missions.prompt: Share your observation about Mara Voss with at least one other Chains member.
missions.answer_format: Write their character name and how they responded.

missions.prompt: Eavesdrop on two different secrets or clues from players outside of House Chains. Secrets shared with you directly/deliberately do not count.
missions.answer_format: Record which two secrets you overheard.

missions.prompt: Identify the player whose bedroom chambers the cellar key was lost in. Then obtain the key by any means necessary and enter the wine cellar to retrieve the requested bottle (a 2003 Merlot). Return it to Ivara along with the cellar key.
missions.answer_format: Enter the name of the player whose bedroom chambers the key was lost in and attach a photo of the wine bottle you retrieved.

characters.name: Velka Saye
characters.title: The Blood Banker
characters.pre_event_info: Brilliant, ravenous, and absolutely certain the room is lucky to have you in it. You and your sister, Ivara Saye, run the Blood Bank the entire Crimson Court depends on — every vial of every house's blood flows through your vaults, and you have turned that flow into an empire. Ivara handles enforcement: the debts, the rules, the people who need frightening. You handle the part that matters: the deals, the leverage, the genius of turning blood into capital and capital into power. Together you are a formidable and faintly combustible duo: Ivara will wage holy war over a misweighed vial, while you let the things that actually matter sort themselves out. They usually do. She calls you reckless, you call her impossible, and you are each right, now and then. Blood is currency, and you are the richest creature in this Court, and you have never once let anyone forget it. You are sharp enough to see everything and far too successful to be bothered by most of it; a discrepancy that would keep a lesser steward awake all night is, to you, a rounding error. You sample your own high-grade stock more freely than is strictly wise — why wouldn't you, when you own it? — and it leaves you flushed, expansive, untouchable, three moves ahead and just occasionally one step too cavalier. In a vampire Court, blood logistics are politics in liquid form. You don't merely play that game; you run it.
characters.post_act1_context: Several weeks ago, Caspian Vael came to your window with an authorization under the Marquess's seal: small blood withdrawals from all five houses, and a vague medical purpose. You clocked it as strange, but waved it through anyway. The paper was clean, the fee was modest, and frankly the affairs of desperate heirs are not your concern; you have a bank to run and far larger veins to bleed. You mentioned it once to Isla Vern, amused more than concerned at the time. You are slightly less amused now. Caspian is missing and for the first time in longer than you can remember, something happened on your watch that you did not trouble yourself to control. It is not guilt — you don't really do guilt. But you are calculating how to make sure no one pins his disappearance on you, and whether, even now, there is some profit to be made from it.
secrets: Several weeks ago, Caspian Vael came to me with an authorization under the Marquess's seal, requesting small blood withdrawals from all five houses, for "medical purposes." I clocked it as strange and processed it anyway. The seal was good, the matter beneath my notice, and I do not have time to interrogate every desperate fool who comes to my window. I signed his blood out across five houses and joked with Isla Vern afterward about whether the heir was truly ill or merely playing hooky. With Caspian gone tonight, my little joke to Isla has aged rather poorly. A pity; he was good for business, and the dead rarely settle their accounts.

A few months back I was on my way down to the cellar when I caught Valen Drear and Magistra Moore in what I can only describe as an exceptionally strange exchange. He stood there working at something he couldn't get out, as though the words were stuck behind his teeth, and when he finally gave it up and walked off, her whole face had changed. I would have forgotten it entirely, except a few nights later I found her in the archive past any reasonable hour, copying pages by hand: Crimson Codex entries, the Statutes on voiding an illegitimate succession. People do not lose sleep transcribing law for sport. She is building something against someone — and I should rather like to know who, since in this Court that sort of knowledge tends to be worth a great deal.

missions.prompt: Work the phrase “I’m rich, you see” into three separate conversations as the explanation for your opinion, behavior, or lack of concern.
missions.answer_format: Write the three character names and what you were explaining at the time.

missions.prompt: Every blood vial represents power. Sell your secret concerning Caspian’s large, suspicious blood withdrawal to at least two players for at least 1 BT each.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of the characters you transacted with

missions.prompt: Launch a blood-backed investment vehicle to acquire a high-value relic. Recruit at least five investors from your house, each of whom must contribute at least one BT. Use the pooled capital to purchase or acquire a relic from the Reliquary. After the purchase, explain to your investors how the fund is structured, who holds equity, who gets voting rights, and why you, obviously, retain management control.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the fund, the five investors, their contributions, the relic acquired, and who you claim has controlling interest.

characters.name: Wisteria Pierce
characters.title: The Matchmaker
characters.pre_event_info: Elegant, perceptive, and impossible to scandalize, you arrange introductions for the Court. Some become partnerships, or even marriages. Others become feeding arrangements, patronage contracts, or private disasters no one ever admits were your doing. Your clients rarely say what they want directly. They ask for compatibility, discretion or companionship. Usually, they mean something less polite: hunger, loneliness, or someone they cannot be seen wanting.They come to you because you understand the desire beneath the euphemism. You do not judge these appetites. You catalog them, arrange them, and protect them.
characters.post_act1_context: You never expected to find yourself entangled in succession politics. With Caspian gone, everyone suddenly wants to know about his personal life. Most are asking the wrong questions. You are one of the few people who knew of Lena and met her in person. Several weeks ago she asked you whether vampires could ever be compelled to act against their will. At the time you assumed she was worried about love, loyalty, or the dangers of immortality. Now, with Caspian missing and the Court in turmoil, you suspect she may have been asking about something else entirely.
secrets: Several months ago, Caspian Vael came to you with a delicate request: he wished to communicate discreetly with a mortal woman named Lena. At first, you assumed it was a passing indulgence. You were wrong. The letters became meetings. It started off slowly, but soon they were meeting weekly. She would come to the gate, patiently, waiting to be turned away. He’d watch until she left, then sneak off to meet her.
Lena once asked me whether blood could be used to control another vampire. I dismissed the question at the time. I’m no longer sure it was hypothetical.

missions.prompt: Do what you do best: make a new match! Find two players you think would be good together and approach them both, suggesting that they plan a date.
missions.answer_format: Enter the character names of the two players you attempted to set-up on a date.

missions.prompt: Find one person in search of a specific piece of knowledge or item, and another person who knows/has that specific piece of knowledge or item. Introduce the players and help them draw the connection.
missions.answer_format: Enter the names of the players and the piece of knowledge or item that ties them together.

missions.prompt: Discover what happened to Lena after Caspian disappeared. Determine whether she fled, was taken, was silenced/killed, went into hiding, or is still pursuing the truth.
missions.answer_format: Enter what you believe happened to Lena.

characters.name: Mirabel Ashvane
characters.title: The Gossip
characters.pre_event_info: You are a long-standing fixture of the Crimson Toast circuit — a Court socialite who has attended every Toast for the last few centuries and is personally acquainted with at least one member of every house. You have no official role; your value is purely social, and it is considerable. You know everyone's business and you cannot help sharing it. Sharp-tongued, socially fearless, and genuinely warm beneath the chatter. Where Isla Vern is the person people choose to confide in, you are the person who finds things out whether anyone chooses to tell you or not. You see, you notice, and you talk — loudly, colorfully, and immediately. People find this charming. Occasionally they find it catastrophic. You have never much cared which.
characters.post_act1_context: Your old friend Orren Mast, the groundskeeper, confided in you recently that he had fallen for the mortal woman at the gate — and was bitter to discover her heart belonged to Caspian. Now Caspian has failed to appear, and you cannot help wondering about the edge in Orren's voice when he spoke of him. Separately, yesterday evening you saw a member of House Cinders moving through the estate carrying several small dark vials. He avoided eye contact and changed direction when he saw you. You don't know what was in those vials, but have wondered if they contained some kind of poison.
secrets: Orren Mast and I were involved once, years ago. It ended quietly and we stayed friendly; he is one of the few who still talks to me like a person rather than a source. A few weeks ago he confided that he had taken a liking to the mortal woman who kept coming to the gate, and was stung to learn her heart belonged to Caspian. He spoke of Caspian with a bitterness I had not heard from him before. Now Caspian has failed to appear tonight, and I find myself wondering how far that bitterness could have taken him.

Yesterday evening I saw a member of House Cinders moving through the estate carrying several small dark vials. He avoided eye contact and changed direction when he saw me. I don't know what was in those vials, but have been wondering if they contained a poison of sorts. I plan to tell others what I saw.

Late last night I could not sleep and saw Petra Nox, the Marquess's own bodyguard, moving Brother Aldric Voss through the estate in the wee hours. I don’t believe Brother Aldric was going willingly. I do not know where Petra took him or why, only that he has not been seen since. I have been deciding who to tell, and how much.

missions.prompt: Introduce yourself in character to at least one member of every house before Act 2 ends.
missions.answer_format: Enter one character name from each house you spoke with.

missions.prompt: Spread the gossip that Orren shared with you (his lust for the mortal women and bitterness towards Caspian) to at least two players outside of Spires, and spread the story of what you saw yesterday (the guest with dark vials) to at least two additional non-Spires players. These four players must be different players than those who you introduced yourself to in Mission 1. Note their reactions.
missions.answer_format: Enter all four character names, which secret to you shared with each, and one word describing each person's reaction.

missions.prompt: By Act 3, be able to name one piece of private information — a secret, observation, or relic — held by at least one player from each of the five houses. You don't need to have shared it. You just need to know it.
missions.answer_format: Enter one character name per house and the piece of information you learned from or about them.

characters.name: Sebastian Rouge
characters.title: The Blood Artist
characters.pre_event_info: Magnetic, rapturous, and utterly devoted to your craft. You are the most coveted portraitist in the vampiric world — your canvases hang in every great house, and patrons wait years and pay fortunes for the privilege of sitting beneath your gaze. There is something in your work no rival can touch: a heat, an intimacy, as though the subject might draw breath and step from the frame. To sit for you is to be adored, and your subjects leave feeling more delightfully undead than they have in centuries. You do not paint for coin, though the coin is considerable; you paint because creation is the only passion that has never once disappointed you. To this Court you are a treasure — a flatterer, a seducer, a maker of beautiful things. You let them believe that is all you are.
characters.post_act1_context: You are frequently lauded by those you paint for your ability to make them feel seen in a way they've never felt before. You hold back a knowing smile as they gush, for there is more behind those words than they realize. Into every portrait you paint, you work a single drop of your subject's blood, unseen, and through that bond your art becomes something far deeper and stranger. The painted eyes become your eyes: whenever the living subject comes near the portrait you made of them, you can see them through it, watching from within the frame as though you stood in the room yourself. You have spent this evening as you always do — receiving admirers, fielding commissions, drinking in the beauty of a room full of vampires at their most composed. But that composure has cracked tonight, and there is nothing more beautiful to you than a beautiful thing coming apart. The missing heir, the scramble for the throne, the fear pressing at every flawless face: it stirs you. Frightened people sit so honestly. They commission portraits to feel permanent in an uncertain hour, and they hold so very still beneath your brush. There has never been a richer night for your work, and already your eye has settled, hungrily, on one or two faces you would give almost anything to capture.
secrets: Marquess Gruber's portrait hangs in her private cellar, and through its eyes I have watched her there. She descends alone, at odd hours, and draws out a single bottle she keeps apart from all the rest — and does something to it I can never quite make out before she seals it away again. I do not know what it means. I only know that a woman with nothing to hide does not visit her own cellar like a thief in the night.

I painted Mara Voss this past season, and for the first time in my life, my art failed me. I worked her blood into the portrait as I always do, yet no bond ever formed — no line of sight, as though something already held its claim on her and left no room for mine. I have painted hundreds and bound them all to my works. I cannot stop turning it over: what is she, or what has been done to her, that even my craft slides off her like water from glass? It unsettles me more than I will admit.

My work demands blood — far more than any sitter would willingly give. So all season I have been quietly drawing from the ceremonial stores, a vial here and there, never enough to be missed, or so I believed. If anyone has noticed the counts running short, let them look to thieves and cellar rats. The blood I take feeds the only thing in this Court worth preserving: my art. I will not apologize for it, and I will not confess it.

missions.prompt: You see what others miss. Approach two players and tell each, in your most rapturous voice, the single most beautiful thing about them.
missions.answer_format: Enter the two players and what you admired in each.

missions.prompt: Your art has never failed you as it did with Mara Voss. Find at least one player who knows Mara well and learn what you can about her — what changed in her, who she keeps close to, etc. — to understand why your craft slid off her like water from glass.
missions.answer_format: Enter who you spoke with and what you learned about Mara.

missions.prompt: A collector is measured by their collection. Visit the Reliquary and acquire the Blood Extractor (1 BT), then persuade two different players to sit for a portrait: a sketch or quick painting, your choice. As each sitting ends, discreetly "extract" a drop of their blood with your tool (a stealthy, harmless poke — no one is actually drained!) to complete the binding.
missions.answer_format: Enter the character names of the two players you captured and poked, and submit a photo of each portrait.

characters.name: Ivara Saye
characters.title: The Flame-Speaker
characters.pre_event_info: Zealous, eerie, and genuinely convinced that vampire law must be upheld above all else. You run the Blood Bank — the Court's central repository for tracking ceremonial blood flows across all five houses. Your sister, Velka Saye serves as your cellar master, managing the physical stores while you oversee the formal ledger and requisition process. Your role gives you visibility into every sanctioned blood transaction in the Court. You have been using that visibility to track something unsanctioned for months.
characters.post_act1_context: You had already noticed the blood discrepancy through the Blood Bank records: small withdrawals from all five houses, no official requisition, no ledger entry. Then your close friend Isla Vern mentioned two things in the same conversation: that blood had been signed out across all five houses for Caspian under a false authority claim, and that Caspian had asked her weeks earlier for a discreet contact who could locate obscure information. She had pointed him to Silas Venn. You knew of the Rite of Undoing from your studies. You had long suspected Valen was bound; his degradation was unmistakable.
