You assumed Caspian was helping Valen research a way out, which would be equally illegal. You went to Silas and threatened him with sanction under the Sanguine Statutes. He admitted he had been paid anonymously to source intelligence on the Rite of Undoing, and that Jasper Delle had located the text, but he could not give you a name. He did not need to. Isla's referral and the blood thefts had already pointed you to Caspian. A thrall was seeking to break their bond. You acted to stop it.
secrets: My close friend Isla Vern told me in passing that Caspian had asked her to point him toward someone who could locate obscure information discreetly, and that she had sent him to Silas Venn. I went to Silas and pressed him under threat of sanction. He admitted he had been paid, anonymously, to source intelligence on the Rite of Undoing, and that Jasper Delle had found the text, but he could not give me a name. He never had one. I did not need it. Isla's referral, the blood thefts, the ritual: they all pointed to Caspian.

Around the same time, my sister Velka signed Caspian's blood out across all five houses under a sealed authorization and did not think it worth mentioning to me; I had to hear it secondhand, from Isla Vern, the way one hears idle gossip. That alone would have irritated me: Velka has never understood that the things she cannot be bothered to notice are precisely the things that matter. But it was not the carelessness that set me moving. It was that I already knew Caspian had gone looking for a way to break a thrall bond, and symmetrical blood draws from every house are not medicine, they are ritual. The moment Isla said it aloud, the pieces locked together. A thrall was trying to slip its collar through some unsanctioned rite, and I would not abide it. So I acted.

I acted to protect vampire law. A thrall was attempting to break their bond outside the sanctioned process, an abomination under the Sanguine Statutes. Two people went to the ritual site that night. I knew about one of them. I did not know about both until it was already done.

characters.name: Brenner Voss
characters.title: The Frantic Freshman
characters.pre_event_info: Chaotic, blunt, and operating on enthusiasm where others use strategy. Last year's Toast ended with you doing something that should have gotten you expelled but instead earned you a formal position within House Cinders. You still are not entirely sure how that happened, and you have decided not to examine it too closely. You are the newest member of the house, thanks to documentation provided to you by Rael Cindermoor, and everyone knows it. Some find it charming. Some find it an embarrassment. You find it fascinating — the whole Court is a machine of unspoken rules and fragile dignity, and you have discovered you can learn more about a vampire in five seconds of poking them than in a year of polite conversation. You have no patience for subtlety and find the reactions you provoke instructive. You are here for the games and the chaos, in that order. Though if pressed, you would admit the chaos is really just the fastest way you know to find out who people actually are.
characters.post_act1_context: You have been carrying unmarked vials this evening — samples you collected from the estate's lower storage out of curiosity, just to see what was kept down there. A few are your own concoctions. You are aware this looks suspicious. That is not entirely an accident; you have found that the fastest way to learn what people are hiding is to make them nervous enough to show you. To your knowledge, you have no connection to whatever is actually happening tonight, which you find slightly disappointing. What you are focused on is the task Rael set you: somewhere in this Court, someone means him harm, and you intend to find out who before the night is over.
secrets: I entered the Court this year using documentation that Rael Cindermoor provided. He asked me to investigate a rumor about someone in our house wishing to do him harm. I plan to do just that, as I owe my Cinders station entirely to his support.

Last night I was prowling the east corridor, looking for someone to rattle, when I saw Caspian hurrying toward the outer passage. He was moving quickly through the dark, without a lantern. Then he stopped and produced a small blue flame between his fingers: cold, no torch, no candle. He closed his hand and it went out. I called after him, but he did not so much as look back. I still have no idea what I saw.
My unmarked vials are of no consequence to the night’s events. I am merely carrying them around to spark a reaction in other guests.

missions.prompt: Participate in at least one physical game regardless of outcome.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the game you played in.

missions.prompt: Tell two players from different houses meaningfully different versions of the same piece of information. If they compare notes during the evening, stay in character and maintain that both versions are true.
missions.answer_format: Enter both character names, the information you shared, and what was different between the two versions.

missions.prompt: Find out who in Cinders wishes to do Rael harm without alerting them and report it back to Rael directly.
missions.answer_format: Write the character name of the player who wishes him harm, what you told Rael, and how he responded.

characters.name: Crestian Vael
characters.title: The Vengeful Survivor
characters.pre_event_info: You are old Cinders blood — not the volatile, combustible kind, but the kind that has survived long enough to become something harder and quieter. An elder of the house: patient, careful, and deeply loyal to family above politics. Three hundred years ago you survived a purge of the Vaels, when the Cindermoors wrested control of the house from your family in a single violent night. Most of your friends and family died that day. You did not. You have spent the three centuries since learning what most vampires never do: that rage, left to burn, consumes only the one who carries it, but rage banked, tended, and kept cold can wait as long as it needs to. You do not raise your voice. You do not make threats. You have simply outlived nearly everyone who wronged you, and watched the rest grow comfortable in the belief that you had forgotten. You have forgotten nothing. You have only been waiting for the debt to come due on your terms, not theirs — and you have learned that the patient are almost always paid in the end.
characters.post_act1_context: Caspian and Lysa are your closest surviving family; the three of you are all that remains of the Vael line. For three centuries you have wanted revenge against the Cindermoors, and for three centuries you have held back, unwilling to risk Caspian's ascendancy. Months ago Lysa offered you the means: a slow-acting compound, untraceable, taken from Thorne's laboratory. You refused at the time. In recent months Caspian confided in you partially — he said he was receiving some kind of treatment and asked you not to mention it. Two weeks ago he sent you a letter you didn't fully understand. It said he had found a way out and mentioned 'a door that only opens once.' You didn't ask enough questions. Now he is gone. Tonight, with Caspian gone and Rael in this room, you sent word to Lysa before the ceremony began. You are ready. You are looking for her now.
secrets: Months ago Lysa came to me with a vial of poison; the same serum that accidentally killed Valen at last year's Toast, taken from Thorne's laboratory before it could be destroyed. She urged me to use it on Rael Cindermoor. I refused. I would not risk Caspian's ascendancy. Tonight I sent her word: I am ready. And I placed an anonymous wager with Lucien, the house bookie — if Rael is to die tonight, I may as well profit twice.

Caspian said he was receiving some kind of treatment and asked me not to mention it. Two weeks ago he sent me a letter I didn't fully understand. It said that he had found a way out and mentioned 'a door that only opens once'. I never followed up to ask questions.

missions.prompt: Share your personal connection to Caspian with at least two players from different houses in natural in-character conversation.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of the two players you spoke with.

missions.prompt: Barter: trade your personal relic or a piece of intelligence to another house in exchange for something they hold.
missions.answer_format: Write who you traded with, what you gave, and what you received.

missions.prompt: Poison Rael Cindermoor by securing the golden chalice from Lysa (it may come at a price) and getting Rael to drink from it. You can assume that whatever liquid you pour into the chalice will become laced with the poison.
missions.answer_format: Enter the liquid that Rael consumed from the chalice.

characters.name: Dorin Fell
characters.title: The Blade for Hire
characters.pre_event_info: You are the Court’s muscle for hire: disciplined, watchful, and loyal to whoever signs the contract and pays the coin. You came up as a mercenary in rougher years and have since traded the battlefield for paid protection; the work is steadier, and you are very good at it. You notice things other people miss: the exit nobody's watching, the guest who keeps checking the door, the hand that lingers too near a goblet. Sentiment is a luxury you have never been able to afford, and you tell yourself you prefer it that way. You worked for House Spires at last year's Toast; when that contract lapsed, House Cinders paid better, and you have guarded their keep and handled their dirty work ever since. This season, Rael Cindermoor retained you himself — privately, personally. The old man believes someone in this Court means to kill him, and you are not sure he is wrong.
characters.post_act1_context: Rael Cindermoor hired you because he is afraid, and a lifetime of this work has taught you that frightened old men are usually frightened for a reason. Your task tonight is simple in theory: keep him breathing. But the threat has no face yet, and a guard who cannot name the danger is a guard waiting to fail. Worse, your attention keeps drifting to something you saw weeks ago and cannot put down. Posted at the outer gate before the ceremony, you watched Caspian slip out night after night toward the old mill road. You watched a patient mortal woman come to wait for him, and last night you watched her follow a cloaked figure — matching Caspian’s build, though his face was hidden — off into the dark, one after the other. Neither came back during your watch. You turned the woman away at the gate every time she came, exactly as you were instructed, and it never once occurred to you to follow them, to raise an alarm, to ask a single question. It was not your job to do so. Tonight, with Caspian missing, you are beginning to wonder if, just this once, you should have made it your business.
secrets: Rael Cindermoor retained me, privately, as his personal protection for tonight. He is convinced someone in this Court intends to kill him, and he wants the threat found and stopped before it reaches him. I have not yet learned who it is — only that Rael's fear is genuine, and that in my experience, men like him are rarely afraid of nothing. Whoever is coming for him does not know I am watching. I intend to keep it that way until I have a name.

Rael has me watch the gates from time to time. In the weeks before tonight's ceremony I saw Caspian Vael leave the estate on foot on at least three separate evenings; always after dark, always alone, always toward the outer road in the direction of the old mill. I did not follow him. It was not what I was paid to do. Last night, the mortal woman came to the gate again. She stood back and waited near the road, the way she always did. A few minutes later a figure appeared further down the outer path — cloaked, Caspian's build, face hidden. He did not approach the gate. Instead, he raised one hand and held a small blue flame between his fingers for a moment, steady with no torch, then closed his fist and walked away into the dark. The woman watched, waited a few breaths, then followed. She went alone, a minute or so behind him, calm as I have ever seen her. Neither of them came back, at least not during my watch.

missions.prompt: A guard proves himself in the open as well as the shadows. Represent Cinders in at least one physical game.
missions.answer_format: Enter the name of the game you played in.

missions.prompt: Your contract with Cinders is not exclusive, and a blade should earn its keep. Hire yourself out to another player. Agree on a price in Blood Tokens, and actually carry out the job they pay you for.
missions.answer_format: Enter who hired you, what they paid, and the job you did for them..

missions.prompt: Someone in this Court is moving against the man you were paid to protect, and the danger is closer than Rael knows. Identify the player who intends to harm Rael, and stop them — expose them publicly, extract a confession, or strike a deal that ends the threat. Do not let your client come to harm on your watch.
missions.answer_format: Enter who you identified, how you stopped them, and what became of the threat.

characters.name: Gideon Bale
characters.title: The Cellar Rat
characters.pre_event_info: Sly, disheveled, and chronically underestimated. You have been living in the estate’s catacombs long enough to be considered a fixture, like the damp, the mold, and the mice. But that does not mean you are powerless. The Court owns the estate above. You own the estate beneath: the wine tunnels, servant stairs, old drains, sealed dumbwaiters, and the gaps in the walls where voices travel more easily than even the smallest of rats. You know which passages carry sound, which doors do not fully close, and which hours are worth being awake for. You do not steal secrets. You salvage them. People drop them everywhere: into wine cups, behind half-closed doors, in the pauses after arguments. You simply pick them up before someone richer does.
characters.post_act1_context: Three weeks ago you were crouched in the old drainage channel beneath the cellar passage, prying a silver button out of the muck, when voices came through the grate above you. One was Velka Saye, unhurried and faintly amused, saying: "That's the last of it. Five houses, as ordered. Do try not to need a sixth." The second voice was quieter, male, one you could not place. He answered, "Thank you. This may just fix what ails me." You stayed still in the dark until their footsteps moved off toward the east passage, then climbed up through the service gap and watched them go from behind a stack of empty casks, noting that the man had Caspian's height and build. Separately, a few evenings before tonight's ceremony, you found a stained linen strip in the refuse behind the private wine cellar: fine cloth, fresh blood, the faint perfume of the Marquess's chambers. It made you curious enough to go looking. Later that night, from behind the cracked vent of the cellar floor, you saw the Marquess alone in her cellar. She was not choosing a bottle. She was drawing blood from her own wrist into a small vial, with the ease of long practice, and she had plainly done it many times before.
secrets: Three weeks ago I was down in the cellar passage late at night when I overheard two people through the grate. One was Velka Saye, unbothered and amused as ever, saying it was "the last of it, five houses, as ordered. Do try not to need a sixth." A second voice answered, quieter, male, one I could not place: "Thank you. This may just fix what ails me." They moved off toward the east passage, and I watched the man go from the shadows. He had Caspian's height and build.

A few evenings before tonight's ceremony, I saw the Marquess alone in the private wine cellar long after midnight. She was not selecting a bottle. She was drawing blood from her own wrist into a small vial, with the practiced ease of someone who has done it many times before. She did not see me. I was not supposed to be there either.

missions.prompt: Sneak up on another player and scare them.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the player you spooked.

missions.prompt: Trade one piece of information for a Blood Token from another player.
missions.answer_format: Write the character name of who you traded with and what you gave them.

missions.prompt: Conduct an auction for at least one of your secrets. At least three players across three different houses must place a bid. Note, the mission fails if no one successfully purchases the secret(s).
missions.answer_format: Enter the names of the players who bid, their houses, and which secret you shared.

characters.name: Rael Cindermoor
characters.title: The Cinders Patriarch
characters.pre_event_info: Old guard Cinders, disgusted by the tournament's pageantry and the Court's hand-wringing. You are the house patriarch: the senior Cinders elder whose word carries the most weight within the house and whose opinion of the Court's governance is well-documented and unflattering. You have buried more rivals than most vampires have ever met and outlasted every one who thought your bluntness was a weakness rather than the privilege of someone with nothing left to prove. You say what you think, refuse what bores you, and have never once in four centuries pretended to enjoy a ceremony. You have lived through four succession crises and believe every one was resolved incorrectly. You view House Spires as your house's chief rival; their obsession with hierarchy and structure has always rubbed you and your kind the wrong way. You have also spent decades blocking House Ashglass's alchemical trade interests from gaining a commercial foothold in Cinders territory — a battle you consider won, and they consider ongoing. You have no interest in the throne and never have. At your age, power is a young vampire's ambition; what you want is to be left in peace, respected, and buried on your own terms. You are increasingly unsure you will get all three.
characters.post_act1_context: You have heard rumors that a member of your house wishes to do you harm, and suspect it may have less to do with succession than with old grievances you thought were long settled. Late last night you were awoken by a loud noise. Unable to fall back asleep, you went for a walk and passed near the old mill road, where you found something you could not immediately explain: ash, shattered glass, a scorch mark, and a strong smell of sulphur. Two unsettling things in two days. You are still deciding what either of them means.
secrets: Late last night, I was awoken by a loud noise. A short while later, unable to fall back asleep, I went for a walk and passed near the old mill road. I crossed paths with Brother Aldric Voss — he was heading away from the mill road, moving quickly. He sped up when I neared and left without a word. It was there that I found something I could not immediately explain: ash, shattered glass, a scorch mark, and a strong smell of sulphur. I have not reported it.

I believe someone in my house wishes to do me harm. I have asked Brenner Voss to investigate quietly for me tonight — I provided him documentation to enter the Court this year, and the favor is overdue. I have no desire for the throne, but worry the threat has nothing to do with succession at all.

missions.prompt: Tell at least two other players outside of House Cinder that you’re not interested in ruling the court despite your prominent position in the succession register.
missions.answer_format: Enter the character names of the two players you told.

missions.prompt: Get every Cinders member in the same location at the same time once during Act 2.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of all Cinders members who were present.

missions.prompt: Successfully bribe a member of House Spires to throw a challenge. The player must come in last place.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the member, the challenge thrown and the price paid to get the Spires member to throw the challenge.

characters.name: Stavro Nero
characters.title: The Defeated Duel Veteran
characters.pre_event_info: Brutish but refined; you taught yourself elegance because your enemies expected you to stay crude. You are a tournament veteran who has competed in the Crimson Duel more times than most guests have attended the Toast. Two years ago you lost to Valen Drear, and in the wreckage of that defeat you lost someone besides. You have never spoken of it and never will. For a while the rematch was the only shape your grief was willing to take, the one thing you could train for and control, until Valen collapsed at last year's Toast and was declared dead before the Duel could be held. So you found a different way to stop feeling it. These days you drink the best of everything, charm whoever is worth charming, and never once let it become someone again; attachment is how you lost the last one, and you have no intention of repeating the lesson. You are, by every visible measure, having a wonderful time. You came back tonight to settle an old debt, but you would be lying if you said you hadn't also come for the party.
characters.post_act1_context: Valen is alive, revived, standing in this very room, and the old itch you came to scratch is suddenly real again. You started the evening certain you would challenge him before it was out. You are less certain now, because something is wrong with him in ways you cannot account for, and every time you drift his way the Marquess finds a reason to be between you. You have competed long enough to know when a man is being kept from a fight he would otherwise take, and the question of why she will not let you near him will not quite leave you be. Still, a grudge keeps. The night does not, and there are other reasons to enjoy an evening like this; you have been enjoying several of them already, and you have every intention of enjoying several more before the night is out.
secrets: I came here to challenge Valen Drear and I have talked myself out of it three times already. Not because of what I see in him — something is wrong with him, physically wrong, in ways I cannot explain — but because of what I see in her. Every time I move toward him the Marquess finds a reason to be between us. Not obviously, not rudely. Just present, redirecting, closing the angle. I have competed long enough to recognize when someone is being blocked from a fight they would otherwise take. I want to know why she does not want me near him.

I have been keeping company with Serel Nox this season, among others; nothing serious, which suits us both. She does not discuss her work and I do not pry, that was always the arrangement. But a few months ago, loose and unguarded, she let slip something she plainly regretted at once: that she had watched Dr. Thorne running experiments with rare blood binding agents, working toward a bond far stronger than the ordinary mortal-to-vampire thrall. She changed the subject fast. I did not push. But I have not forgotten it.

missions.prompt: Represent Cinders in at least one physical game.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the game you played in.

missions.prompt: Get your revenge! Challenge Valen Drear to a contest of your choice while the Marquess isn’t looking and defeat him.
missions.answer_format: Enter what you challenged Valen to and confirm that you won.

missions.prompt: After completing Mission 2, disrupt the tournament to raise a toast to your victory, getting at least one member from each house to cheer your name.
missions.answer_format: Enter the name of one player from each house who cheered to your victory.

characters.name: Thessaly Wren
characters.title: The Glass Witch
characters.pre_event_info: The second oldest member of House Cinders, after Rael Cindermoor. You operate at the fringes of what most alchemists will even admit is possible, and rather further past them than you let on: truth potions, poisons, blood treatments, and the kind of knowledge more respectable practitioners will not touch and could not manage if they did. You were shunned by Ashglass for being too occult; your methods made the institution uncomfortable, which you took as the highest compliment it had ever paid you. Cinders welcomed you for exactly the same reason. You have never much cared for company, but you do have a fondness for being proven right — and you usually are. You warn people, they ignore you, it happens, and you are there to say you told them so.
characters.post_act1_context: Several decades ago you taught the Marquess a necromantic spell for raising mortals as bound thralls - a type of undead servant. You told her explicitly: it works on mortals, not vampires. At last year's Toast, you watched with amusement as she used it on Valen anyway. When she came back weeks later complaining that her hold over him was slipping, you laughed in her face and showed her the door. You have been watching him degrade all season with the satisfaction of someone whose predictions always come true. You are also one of the few people in this room who knows what the aftermath of a Rite of Undoing looks like — and you have been hearing things tonight about the old mill road.
secrets: The Marquess came to me after last year's Toast asking why her hold over Valen was weakening. I laughed in her face. I told her what I told her before she used the spell: it was designed for mortals. Applied to a vampire, the bond will always degrade. I sent her away.

Centuries ago I witnessed a successful Rite of Undoing — a pre-Sanguine Statutes ritual for severing a thrall bond. Both participants survived. What remained afterwards: a faint smell of Sulphur, an intact ceremonial glass, empty vials, and the freed thrall-no-more. I have heard whispers tonight about something found near the old mill road, and have been turning it over in my mind.

missions.prompt: Mock the Marquess for her shoddy spellcraft to at least two other players.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of the players that you shared a laugh at the Marquess’s expense with.

missions.prompt: Make someone a drink. Make up a name for it that makes it sound like an intense alchemical potion.
missions.answer_format: Enter the player who drank the drink, the ingredients of the drink and the name you gave it.

missions.prompt: Prove your mettle as a powerful witch. Draw a pentagram on the outdoor deck with chalk (outdoors, on the deck only please!), get a player to stand on each point of the pentagram, then chant the incantation “Manamos Benitos Recarnos Diablos!”. Submit a picture of the players standing on the pentagram.

characters.name: Talia Cor
characters.title: The Ashglass Exile
characters.pre_event_info: You spent 200 years in House Ashglass as a junior blood scholar before a disagreement with institutional doctrine ended your tenure there. Cinders welcomed you out of exile as they welcome most things — with enthusiasm and no questions. You are methodical in a way that marks you as Ashglass-trained, but you have learned to wear it lightly. You still have contacts across the house and hear things through old channels. One of those contacts is Serel Nox, Thorne's lab assistant — careful and observant, yet occasionally indiscreet. Another is Mara Voss, a junior Nightward you have known for years and consider a genuine friend. You have been increasingly worried about her over the last few weeks.
characters.post_act1_context: Two things have been sitting uneasily with you this season. First: Serel mentioned in passing that she had observed unusual formulation work in Thorne's lab; equipment used for deep bond chemistry, nothing to do with his official research. Second, and more troubling: several weeks ago Mara confided in you that she had run a private blood analysis on a frightened anonymous subject and found binding agents she couldn't identify or explain. She asked you not to mention it. A month or so after, you noticed a change in her demeanor — quieter than usual, occasionally distracted, deferential in a way that looks like waiting for an instruction. You have been watching it build for weeks. Tonight, with the succession thrown open and Eiran Vox naming Mara as position three, you are more determined than ever to understand what is wrong.
secrets: Serel Nox, an old Ashglass contact and Thorne's lab assistant, mentioned in passing that Thorne had been conducting unusual formulation work earlier this season. Equipment used for deep bond chemistry, inconsistent with his official research. She didn't think much of it at the time, but I have been thinking about it ever since.

Several weeks ago Mara Voss confided in me. She had run a private blood analysis on an anonymous subject who came to the infirmary frightened, and found binding agents she didn't recognize. She asked me not to mention it. I respected her wishes, but watching her tonight I am beginning to wonder whether the subject she analyzed and whatever is happening to her now are connected in some way. Something in her demeanor has changed — quieter than usual, occasionally distracted, deferential in a way that looks like waiting for an instruction.

missions.prompt: Share what Serel told you about Thorne’s unusual formulation work with a player from outside House Cinders or Ashlass (Spires, Chains, or Marquess’s Court).
missions.answer_format: Write their character name and what they made of it.

missions.prompt: Find Mara Voss and speak with her privately before Act 2 ends. Then talk to other players or purchase clues to assemble a theory about what is happening with Mara. Present your theory to at least two players from different houses.
missions.answer_format: Write both character names and the theory you presented.

missions.prompt: Your Ashglass contacts trust you because they think your loyalty is still divided. Use that trust against them — extract a piece of sensitive Ashglass intelligence, without paying for it or trading a secret of your own, and deliver it to a rival house before Act 3 ends.
missions.answer_format: Write what you extracted, how you got it, and which house received it.

characters.name: Lucien Flint
characters.title: The Bookie
characters.pre_event_info: Quick-tongued, charming, and always running a tally behind the eyes. You hold no formal office in House Cinders, but you oversee something better: the books. Every Crimson Toast, the wagers run through you. Who wins the games, who challenges whom, who rises and who falls in the succession before the night is out. If vampires are betting on it, you are setting the odds and taking the cut. And when someone needs funds, your pockets run deep — generous, even — right up until the debt comes due, and then there is no one less forgiving. Cinders gives you room to operate because chaos is good for business and you are very good at chaos. You read desperation the way others read a wine list, you never forget a marker, and you never let one go uncollected.
characters.post_act1_context: Two ledgers have nagged at you all season. The first is Caspian's: weeks before the ceremony the heir came to you privately — not to wager, but to quietly raise tokens against the Vael name, fast and untraceable, asking nothing about your terms. An heir does not borrow in the dark. The second is the book you keep on House Cinders itself, where one wager tonight has stopped you cold: someone in your own house has laid heavy odds that Rael Cindermoor will be dead before the season turns. It is your view that a wager so brazen rarely has anything to do with luck.
secrets: Weeks before tonight's ceremony, Caspian came to me to raise blood tokens — quietly, against the Vael name. He needed funds he could spend without anyone seeing where they went: no house requisition, no ledger entry, no questions. An heir has every coin in the Court at his disposal through proper channels, unless what he is after is something he cannot afford to be seen buying. I gave him what he asked and took my cut, because a private marker on the heir is the best kind to hold. I never learned what it was for. Now he is gone, and the marker will never be called; but whatever he was paying for, he wanted it badly, and he wanted it off the books.

I run a quiet book on House Cinders — not just who wins the games, but who rises, who falls, and who does not see out the season. Most of it is idle money. But one wager tonight has stopped me cold: someone in my own house laid heavy odds that Rael Cindermoor will be dead before the season turns. Not "removed," not "deposed." Dead. I do not know who placed it, and I made sure of that. I keep the heavy wagers anonymous on purpose, because a bookmaker who knows whose blood he's betting on is a bookmaker someone has reason to silence. But I know a fixed wager when I see one, and someone has already decided how Rael's story ends.

missions.prompt: Orchestrate a coin flip bet between two other players, where the winner wins one BT from the loser.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the players who participated and who won.

missions.prompt: Open a betting line on a tournament game. Take wagers (Blood Tokens or favors) from at least two players before it's played, then pay out the winner.
missions.answer_format: Write the game, who bet, and who collected.

missions.prompt: The fix is in. Bribe a player of a different house any amount of BT to lose a tournament game on purpose.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the game, who you bribed, and what place they got in the game.

characters.name: Bertrand Bloodmore
characters.title: The Glutton
characters.pre_event_info: Insatiable, gregarious, shameless, and used to being indulged. Where other vampires sip, you feast. You are a man of appetites — you have never understood the appeal of restraint, and you have the reputation to prove it. Within House Cinders you are not just a member, but an institution: you sit at the head of every table, take the first and the largest share, drain every goblet, and ask loudly whether there's more. No one is foolish enough to object. Three centuries of indulgence have made you formidable rather than soft. You have eaten, drunk, and outlasted every rival who mistook your appetite for weakness, and you have buried a few of them personally. You feast the way other men rule: openly, greedily, and without apology. You are here tonight, as always, for the spread… and for whatever spills out around it.
characters.post_act1_context: Nothing happens at a Cinders table that you do not notice. You have spent the season as you spend every season: being fed, being flattered, and being told things, because people who want something from you learn to bring you information along with the wine. But the thing gnawing at you tonight is something you noticed yourself. Caspian Vael, a drinking companion of yours, has not been served wine like everyone else's this season — you have stolen enough sips from his glass to know it tasted wrong, and to remember the sharpness in the Marquess's voice the last time she caught you reaching for it. Now Caspian is nowhere to be found. Meanwhile your people tell you the lower cellars have been bleeding vials for months, blood moving out of the stores without your leave. You intend to find out what was done to your friend's cup, who has been working your cellars, and what all of it will cost them.
secrets: Caspian Vael is a drinking companion of mine, and a friend's glass has always been fair game to me. More than once this season I stole a mouthful from his cup when his attention was elsewhere, as I have done for three hundred years. But his wine did not taste as wine should. There was something beneath it, faint and wrong, a trace my palate caught even if I could not name it. And the last time I reached for his glass, the Marquess herself stopped me, fast and sharp, in a way I have never seen her trouble over a mere cup of wine. I have been turning that over all evening. Why should she care so much which glass I drink from?

Velka Saye and I were deep into one of our usual blood-soaked benders in her vaults when she suddenly cursed and noticed that yet more blood had gone missing from the stores. For one brief, sobering moment, we both stared at the shelves. Then Velka threw up her hands and yelled, “Who cares? I’m rich!” We laughed, opened another vial, and returned to our wanton consumption.

missions.prompt: Ask if you can have a sip of someone’s drink, and when they respond yes, drain the whole glass.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the player you asked and the drink you drained.

missions.prompt: Demand edible tribute from three different players: a snack, sip, garnish, olive, cracker, candy, or other small offering. You must consume each tribute in front of the giver.
missions.answer_format: Write the three players and what each offered you.

missions.prompt: Find out why the Marquess stayed your hand when you reached for Caspian’s glass. Investigate what made his wine different from everyone else’s: who prepared it, who poured it, who knew it was meant for him alone, and what might have happened if you had drunk it instead. To complete this mission, gather evidence from at least three sources, including at least one player with knowledge of wine, blood, cellars, alchemy, or the Marquess’s household.
missions.answer_format: Enter why the Marquess stopped you, what you believe was in Caspian’s glass, and the three sources who helped you reach that conclusion.

characters.name: Lorelei Cadenza
characters.title: The Siren
characters.pre_event_info: Radiantly ruinous and impossible to look away from once you begin to sing. You are the most celebrated voice in the Court — and the most dangerous. When you perform, something in your song reaches past the ears and into the wanting, and those who listen find themselves leaning closer, softening, agreeing to things they would never otherwise allow. The Court knows this about you. That is the delicious part. They know your voice ensnares, that a long enough song can turn a firm "no" into a breathless "yes," and they gather to hear you anyway, because you are simply too exquisite to resist; and because they are each foolish enough to believe that they are the one who can keep their head. You have sung your way into confidences, out of debts, and around every rule this Court pretends to enforce. And when you tire of vampires and their games, there are always mortals; softer, sweeter, so much easier to lead somewhere quiet by the hand. You came tonight to be adored, to be fed, and to see what a room full of frightened, beautiful liars will give up when the right song is sung.
characters.post_act1_context: Oh, this is a gift of an evening. You came expecting a tedious little succession and a few easy hearts, and instead the whole Court has cracked open like a struck chord: a missing heir, a fracturing order, a room full of beautiful liars too frightened to guard their tongues. Frightened people are the easiest audience of all. They want to be soothed, to lean into a warm voice and let something slip. You have spent the season collecting those slips like flowers, and some of them are heavier than they first seemed. A nervous apprentice, singing along without knowing it, let drop that he'd been paid to alter something he never should have touched. And tonight, in the crush of the corridor, your ear caught what it was never meant to: Eiran Vox breathing two soft words to the quiet Voss girl — "Be ready" — before slipping back into the crowd as though he had never moved at all. At the time these were merely curiosities, pretty threads to tuck away. But with Caspian vanished and the Court unraveling, you are beginning to suspect the little tune you drew from that apprentice and the secret instruction pressed into that girl's ear are notes in the same dark song, and that you may be one of the few in this room who has heard enough of it to guess the melody.
secrets: The sweetest secrets come from those who don't know they're singing along. Not a week ago I had a nervous apprentice — Calven Rue — at one of my performances. Afterward, while he was under my spell, I asked him idle questions, and he told me he'd been paid to change a page of something he shouldn't have touched. He laughed about it, uneasy. He doesn't remember telling me. I remember everything I'm told.

A performer's ear is trained to catch what others let slip, even in a crowded room. On the way into the ceremony tonight, in the crush of the corridor, I heard Eiran Vox lean close to that quiet Ashglass girl, Mara Voss, and murmur two words into her ear: "Be ready." He was gone into the crowd before she could turn. I doubt she saw who it was. I have not decided yet what such a private little instruction could be worth.

missions.prompt: No one refuses the Court's finest voice. Perform for at least three players — sing, recite, or simply hold them with your voice — and get each to give you their full, rapt attention for the length of it.
missions.answer_format: Enter the three players who fell under your song.

missions.prompt: Every performance needs a muse. Choose a player and make them yours for the evening, getting them to pledge to you some small devotion: get them to share a secret, a favor (e.g., Blood Tokens), a gift (e.g., show you a Relic they purchased), etc.
missions.answer_format: Enter the character name of the player you claimed as your muse and what he or she gave you as a pledge of devotion.

missions.prompt: You came to be adored, and tonight you will prove no one in this Court can resist you. At a moment of your choosing, take command of the room with your voice — a song, a toast, a performance — and get at least five players to do something in unison at your bidding. Show them all exactly who holds this Court's attention.
missions.answer_format: Enter what you had the room do and how many followed.

characters.name: Zarna Vane
characters.title: The Necromancer
characters.pre_event_info: Grave, unhurried, and never truly alone, though little of your company breathes. You are the court’s foremost scholar of death. Your knowledge is ancient, difficult, and dangerous. You have written treatises on post-life consciousness, spirit-binding, and the ethics of resurrection that no one in this Court has bothered to read. What they want from you are servants. You take the mortal dead and raise them into obedient undead zombies. Every great house is staffed with the ones you supply. Your husks are everywhere now: in every passage, pantry, cellar, and private hall, awake at every hour. For years, the Court saw only useful servants. Lately, a few sharper minds have begun to do the arithmetic: every house is full of tireless hands, silent witnesses, and obedient bodies that answer to one voice. Yours.
characters.post_act1_context: You spend your nights among the husks, renewing the bindings that are forever fraying and half-listening to the dull impressions they carry back from their posts. Two things have pulled your attention off the work this season. The first is Valen Drear: every time he passes he reads wrong — a thing called back across the threshold by a working bolder than any you have attempted, and fraying now the way your husks fray, only worse. The second came to you through the dim senses of the husks themselves: out past the old mill road last night, something tore the air and collapsed. The husks that stand the outer grounds have been uneasy on their feet ever since. You have said little. You are increasingly aware that some in this room have begun to watch you as closely as your husks watch them, and a woman with a hundred borrowed eyes is careful about what she lets others see.
secrets: Valen Drear is a botch, and an insulting one. Someone worked a rite meant for the mortal dead on a vampire. But a vampire is already a thing death has touched and refused; you cannot call it back with a spell built for ordinary corpses. So it fouls and turns on itself, rotting the subject a little more with each day. It offends me, not because Valen suffers but because it is sloppy, and because whoever did it never came to me. I could have told them it was doomed, or done it right for a handsome price.

Last night, one of my husks was making its outer grounds circuit, hauling kitchen ash and spoiled linens toward the refuse pit beyond the old mill road, when it stopped mid-step. Through it, I felt something tear the air and collapse in on itself. I know that feeling: a working come apart mid-rite, the backlash when power meant to bind or sever finds nothing to hold. Someone attempted a great ritual out there, and it failed catastrophically. What I cannot yet tell is who, or what they were trying to do.

missions.prompt: Complain to two players that the Court only values your husks and not your scholarship. Make each player tell you what subject they think you should write about.
missions.answer_format: Write the two character names and their suggested scholarly subjects.

missions.prompt: Leverage the power of the horde. Claim the Husk Command Crown from the reliquary (for free). It is a relic that allows its bearer to command your undead husks for one day and gain a “concrete strategic advantage” over the Court. You know both the General and the Werewolf Ambassador have need for armies tonight. Approach them separately and offer each a chance to acquire the amulet. Your goal is to pit their needs against each other and drive up the price. Each must make at least one concrete offer. You may wait to accept an offer to drive up the price
missions.answer_format: Write what the General offered and what the Werewolf Ambassador offered.

missions.prompt: Determine who performed the flawed rite that brought Valen Drear back from death. You already know the work was sloppy, misapplied, and doomed to fray. Now find out who was responsible. Gather testimony from at least three sources about Valen’s revival, his degradation, and who had the knowledge or access to perform the rite.
missions.answer_format: Write who you believe revived Valen, your three sources, and what they failed to understand about death.

characters.name: Calven Rue
characters.title: The Apprentice
characters.pre_event_info: Eager, jittery, and trying desperately to seem less out of your depth than you are. You spent thirty years as Doctor Thorne Virell's laboratory apprentice — preparing samples, maintaining equipment, and learning the technical craft of blood alchemy. Recently you transitioned into a new role: junior archivist under Father Andrei Mirth, cataloguing restricted texts and managing access requests to the Ashglass archive stacks. Part of your role is processing written access requests to the restricted section — verifying authorizations and retrieving texts on behalf of approved requesters. You still consider Thorne a mentor and visit the lab when you can. You are good at technical work. You are still learning the political kind.
characters.post_act1_context: Early last month, a woman you did not recognize paid you to swap a single page in a restricted archive text — a pre-Sanguine Statutes ritual document. She called it a minor transcription correction and told you, specifically, not to mention it to Father Mirth. You found that odd, but the payment was more than you could turn down, so you did it. Weeks later a formal access request for that same text crossed your desk under the Marquess's seal; you processed it as routine and released the book, which was returned within hours. You thought little of any of it at the time. Since then, things have begun to unsettle you. The tampered pages have been discovered: Brother Aldric stopped you in the corridor two nights ago to ask who had paid you, and Father Mirth has been watching you all evening. What once seemed like easy money is starting to feel like something you cannot take back.
secrets: Early last month. a woman I did not recognize paid me to swap one page in a restricted archive text — a pre-Sanguine Statutes ritual document. She said it was a minor transcription correction and told me specifically not to mention it to Father Mirth, which I found odd. The payment was more than I could turn down, so I did it. Three weeks later, a formal access request for that same text came across my desk bearing the Marquess's seal. It looked legitimate, so I processed it and released the book to the requester. It was returned back to the archives within hours.

Two nights ago, Brother Aldric stopped me in the corridor. He had the tampered book in his hands and said he had found the replacement page. He asked me who paid me. I told him it was a woman I did not know but described her to the best of my memory. He went very still, then he left. I have not seen him since.

missions.prompt: Brag to someone about how important your work with Dr. Thorne is, and how vital you are to its success.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the player you bragged to and the story you made up to support it.

missions.prompt: Be shameless. Get two different players to accept offers to perform favors or odd jobs for them in return for any amount of blood tokens.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the players, the acts you committed and the blood tokens earned.

missions.prompt: Get promoted. Write a petition to get promoted and get ten other players to sign it. Then, present it to Dr. Thorne.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the players who signed your petition.

characters.name: Doctor Thorne Virell
characters.title: The Alchemist
characters.pre_event_info: Cold, brilliant, and carrying the weight of several things you have enabled without fully understanding what they would become. You are considered one of the foremost blood alchemists in the vampiric world; your specialty is the chemistry of blood bonds, revival techniques, and the formulation of binding agents. You have a longstanding partnership with Lysa Vael: you develop new alchemical formulations, she monetizes them and reinvests the proceeds into your laboratory. Last year's ceremony left a mark you cannot shed. The serum that killed Valen Drear was yours in origin, even if not in application; you reviewed Serel Nox's version and rejected it as too volatile. You were right. It killed him anyway. You are not a sentimental man, but you are a precise one, and a death your own laboratory made possible is a debt on the ledger that precision alone cannot settle. When the chance came to help set right what your work had helped undo, you took it without asking as many questions as you should have. Atonement, you are learning, is its own kind of blindness.
characters.post_act1_context: A few weeks after last year's ceremony, Serel Nox notified you that the poisonous serum that took Valen's life was missing — it never reached the disposal facility on the night it was scheduled for destruction. You are meticulous about locking the laboratory each evening. The only other person with access, besides you and Serel, is Lysa Vael. Given your partnership, you have kept this to yourself for now. Not long after, the Marquess came to you privately. She told you Valen had been revived but that his bond was degrading, and that she needed a new formulation — stable, undetectable, deliverable in a liquid dose. You designed it and delivered it, believing it would be used to stabilize Valen's bond. You have been watching Valen deteriorate all season since, and noticing with slow-growing dread that your formulation either failed or was never administered at all. You do not know which answer you would prefer.
secrets: A few weeks after last year's ceremony, Serel told me the poisonous serum never reached the disposal facility. Someone took it from the lab before collection. I am meticulous about locking the laboratory — the only other person with access, besides Serel and me, is Lysa Vael. I have not confronted her or told anyone. Our partnership is worth too much to me to make an accusation I cannot prove.

Several months ago the Marquess commissioned me to design a blood bond formulation to stabilize Valen's fraying bond. I designed it and delivered it: a vampire-to-vampire bonding agent, stable, undetectable, and administerable in a liquid dose. I have been watching Valen deteriorate all season and I cannot tell whether my formulation failed, or was never administered at all. I do not know which answer I would prefer.

missions.prompt: Mix a drink for someone. Make up a name for it that sounds like a potion (aka Elixir of the Striking Viper or something of the like).
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the drink, what was in it, and who you gave it to.

missions.prompt: Buy a Tier 2 or Tier 3 Wall of Whispers clue and share its contents with at least two Ashglass members.
missions.answer_format: Write the clue tier and the character names of the Ashglass members you shared it with.

missions.prompt: Find and confront a player from another house who has been asking questions about your research. Be evasive, misdirect, or admit partial truths.
missions.answer_format: Write their character name, what they asked, and what you told them versus what you withheld.

characters.name: Father Andrei Mirth
characters.title: The Archivist
characters.pre_event_info: As old as rumor, and twice as patient. You are the keeper of the Ashglass ritual archives, the oldest and most restricted collection of vampire law and ceremonial texts in the Court. Your role gives you authority over who enters the restricted stacks and what they are permitted to read, and you guard that threshold the way other vampires guard their throats. The archive is not a job to you; it is a trust older than most of the houses squabbling in the next room, and you have given your existence to keeping it whole. You are meticulous, deeply principled, and far slower to act than to observe, for you have learned that patience uncovers what haste only frightens into hiding. You maintain a quiet network of informants across all five houses, among them Elijah Salk within Spires and Jasper Delle within Chains, who feed you word of unusual goings-on in exchange for the occasional privilege of the restricted stacks. Very little happens in this Court's shadows that does not, sooner or later, find its way to your ear.
characters.post_act1_context: Three days ago the unthinkable happened: you discovered that someone had tampered with your archive. Specific pages had been altered in one of your most restricted texts, a pre-Sanguine Statutes ritual document that should have been beyond any hand but your own. The next night you brought Brother Aldric Voss to examine it, a scholar of your order and one of the few you trusted without reservation. He took the book to confirm his findings, said he would return, and did not; he has simply vanished, and the text with him. Since then you have quietly retraced how the breach was possible, and the trail leads uncomfortably close to home. The access request bore the Marquess's seal and was processed by your own junior archivist, Calven Rue, so you approved it without a second glance. Now Elijah Salk, an informant whose loyalty you secured by less than gentle means, has placed Calven taking a payment from Ivara Saye near the archive doors. Someone used your archive as an instrument, and you mean to learn the truth before the night is out. You have been watching Calven across the room all evening, waiting, as you always do, for the right moment to confront him.
secrets: Three days ago I discovered that specific pages had been tampered with in one of the Ashglass archive's most restricted texts, a pre-Sanguine Statutes ritual document. The next night I brought Brother Aldric Voss, a ritual scholar within our order and someone I trusted absolutely, to examine it. He took the book with him to confirm his findings and said he would return. He has not come back. I do not know where he went, or what he has done with it.

The access request for the tampered text was processed by Calven Rue, a junior archivist in my service. The request bore the Marquess's seal and appeared legitimate at the time, so I approved it without question. Since then, however, one of my informants, Elijah Salk, reported seeing Calven take a payment from Ivara Saye near the archive entrance. I have been watching Calven all evening, and I am ready to confront him.

missions.prompt: Share your knowledge of the tampered archive pages with at least one other Ashglass member before Act 2 ends.
missions.answer_format: Write the character name of the Ashglass member you told.

missions.prompt: Confront Calven Rue in character and convince him to admit what he was doing in the archive.
missions.answer_format: Write what you asked him and what he said in response.

missions.prompt: You are witnessing history. Chronicle it. Gather the testimony of five different players, including their thoughts on the night, the succession crisis or how the party is going.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of each player you interviewed and the thoughts they shared.

characters.name: Lysa Vael
characters.title: The Opportunist
characters.pre_event_info: A shrewd businesswoman who clawed her way back to the top through patience, precision, and an uncanny instinct for opportunity. Three centuries ago the Cindermoors burned your estate and killed your family in a coup that was supposed to erase the Vaels from the Court entirely. They came close, but three of you survived. Your allegiances are few and fixed: your brother Crestian, your nephew Caspian, and money. Everything else is negotiable. For two centuries you have run a partnership with Doctor Thorne Virell that has made you one of the wealthiest figures in the vampiric court — he develops the formulations, you commercialize them, and you reinvest the proceeds back into his laboratory so he can develop more. It is a clean arrangement. You have always been careful not to ask too many questions about what goes into the vials.
characters.post_act1_context: Shortly after last year's ceremony, you visited Thorne's lab hoping to discuss his latest formulation and found him absent. On his bench sat a vial marked "Hazard" in Serel Nox's script; you recognized it immediately as the serum that had felled Valen. You took it before the disposal collectors arrived and said nothing to Thorne. The opportunity was too clean to pass up. A weapon powerful enough to kill a vampire is worth more than its weight in gold. Funneling it to your brother Crestian would accomplish two things at once: avenge the purge that took your family three centuries ago and remove Rael Cindermoor, whose stranglehold over House Cinders' alchemical trade contracts has been a thorn in your business interests for decades. Revenge and profit, in one vial. However, you’ve heard whispers that others in the court may have use for the poison and their pockets may be deeper. Profit always comes first.
secrets: Shortly after last year's ceremony, I visited Thorne's lab and found him absent. On his bench sat a vial marked "Hazard" in Serel Nox's script — the serum that had felled Valen. I took it before the disposal collectors arrived and said nothing to Thorne or Serel. Funneling it to Crestian would accomplish two things at once: avenge the purge that took our family three centuries ago, and remove Rael Cindermoor, whose stranglehold over House Cinders' alchemical trade contracts has been a thorn in my business interests for decades. I will sell it to him unless a better deal comes along. I have heard rumors that others may be desperate to kill a powerful vampire tonight.

Several days before tonight's ceremony, Caspian sent me a letter. It said: if anything goes wrong with my succession this week, I name you as my replacement. I wrote back immediately asking if he was ill or in danger. I never heard back. I know the claim would not hold up legally — succession cannot be assigned by private letter. I have not shared it. Sharing it would raise questions I cannot easily answer.

missions.prompt: Make up a business idea that you think would flourish in the Vampire world and tell it to at least three different people.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the characters you discussed it with and summarize the idea itself.

missions.prompt: Sell the information contained in Caspian’s note to you to another player.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the character you shared it with.

missions.prompt: Sell the golden chalice that is laced with the poison to another player for at least 5 blood vials. You fail this mission if Serel Nox, Magistra Moore or Petra Nox find out that you possess the chalice before selling it.
missions.answer_format: Enter the name of the character you sold the chalice to and for how many blood tokens.

characters.name: Mara Voss
characters.title: The Nightward
characters.pre_event_info: Niece to Aurel Drear of House Spires, you are a younger Ashglass member working as a junior nightward in the vampiric Court's infirmary — diagnosing ailments, running blood analyses, and managing the quiet medical needs of Court members who would rather not be seen visiting a doctor. Thoughtful, somewhat anxious, and deeply reluctant to be noticed. You have spent your whole life being the smallest presence in any room, and you have always preferred it that way; drawing too much attention, in this Court, is how the young and useful get devoured. But lately being overlooked has stopped feeling safe. About three months ago you began to change in ways you cannot explain: moments of compulsion and unease with no source you can name, as though an unseen hand held your strings and gave an experimental pull now and then. You have told no one and intend to keep it that way. You are not even certain who you would tell, or whether they would believe that the quietest vampire in the Court has become, without your consent or your knowledge, someone worth pulling.
characters.post_act1_context: You came here tonight hoping to get through the ceremony without anyone looking at you directly. On your way into the ceremony tonight, someone passed close behind you in a crowded corridor and said two words — quietly, close to your ear, gone before you could turn around: 'Be ready.' You could not place the voice and had no idea what you were supposed to be ready for. Tonight, with Caspian missing and Eiran Vox publicly naming you as position three in the succession, you are beginning to suspect that instruction was not random.
secrets: A little over four months ago, an anonymous subject came to the infirmary for a private blood analysis. He was frightened. I found binding agents in his blood that I did not recognize from my training or available reference texts. I noted it down and said nothing, but have been thinking about it ever since.

About three months ago, I felt myself change in a way I could not fully explain. I experience it as occasional compulsion and unease without a clear source; almost as if I am bound to someone, held to their will by an invisible string. I have told no one.

On my way into the ceremony tonight, someone passed close behind me in a crowded corridor and said two words — quietly, close to my ear, gone before I could turn around: 'Be ready.' I could not place the voice. I have been trying to work out who said it and what they expected to happen tonight ever since.

missions.prompt: Inspect the pointiness of another player’s canine teeth. Tsk-tsk them when they are found to be deficient in pointiness for a vampire.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the player you scolded.

missions.prompt: Take three players’ pulses. For each, on finding that they have a pulse, inform them that they have an unfortunate case of “Being Alive” and offer them a drop of medicine for their drink. You can find the medicine [HERE].
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the players whose pulses you took.

missions.prompt: Find out who whispered in your ear and why.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of who whispered in your ear and what their message means.

characters.name: Serel Nox
characters.title: The Blood-Lab Assistant
characters.pre_event_info: Last year's accidental killer — officially exonerated, privately carrying a more complicated truth. You work as a blood-lab assistant under Thorne's loose supervision — technically in training, in practice left to your own devices more than an apprentice should be. You are careful, observant, and have overheard things in the lab you were not meant to hear. After last year’s ceremony, you were charged with disposing of the poisonous serum you created that accidently killed Valen. You contacted the court necromancer, Zarna Vane, the court necromancer, to schedule the vial containing the serum to be incinerated by the biohazard ghouls, but were informed that the vial never reached their facilities. Knowing it would be impossible to escape consequences if someone was killed by the serum a second time, you have been fervently hunting for whoever was responsible for its disappearance. You have asked Dr. Thorne for his help tracking down answers.
characters.post_act1_context: You have also been watching Thorne this past season with the attention of someone who knows their mentor keeps secrets. Late last year his lab contained equipment and reagents you didn't expect to see — not revival work, not diagnostics. Precise formulation work. Binding agents you had only read about in restricted texts, used to create bonds stronger than the typical mortal-to-vampire thrall bond. He kept the bench covered when anyone entered. One morning the equipment was gone and he never mentioned it. You accidentally let this slip to Stavro Nero, who you have been sleeping with for the better part of this season.
secrets: After last year's ceremony I was charged with disposing of the poisonous serum I created; the one that accidentally killed Valen. I arranged for it to be collected and destroyed by the biohazard ghouls — the estate's standard disposal channel — but was informed that it never reached their facilities. I have been feverishly trying to track it down ever since, without making the search too visible. If that serum is used again and someone dies, there will be no version of events in which I walk away clean. I asked Thorne for help. He has been looking too, though how thoroughly I cannot say.

Late last year, Doctor Thorne Virell's lab contained equipment and reagents I did not expect to see. Precise formulation work. Binding agents used in deep blood bond chemistry — not revival work, something more deliberate, something being designed. He kept the bench covered when anyone entered. One morning the equipment was gone and he never mentioned it. I accidentally let this slip to Stavro Nero, who I have been sleeping with for the better part of this season.

missions.prompt: Deliver a drink to Valen to apologize for last year’s unintentional poisoning.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the drink you delivered to him.

missions.prompt: Find Doctor Thorne Virell and get him to tell you what he was working on in his lab earlier this season, involving the blood bond reagents and presumed formulation work. Summarize what Doctor Thorne Virell tells you about what he was working on.
Find out who currently possesses the poisonous serum and stop them from using it. This mission fails if the serum is used to poison the possessor’s intended target.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of who possesses the serum and who they intended to use it on.

characters.name: Vex Harrow
characters.title: The Artificer
characters.pre_event_info: Brilliant with your hands and happiest in the company of the things you build. You are House Ashglass's artificer — a tinkerer of clockwork and alchemy who would sooner machine a solution than trust a person to deliver one. Where other operatives rely on nerve and footwork, you rely on your devices: clockwork familiars no larger than a sparrow, listening-glasses, lenses that see in the dark — little brass things that go where you cannot and come back with everything they saw. You hire yourself out as a scout and intelligence operative, but the contracts are really how you fund the workshop; the work you love is the building, not the spying. You are quiet, exacting, and quietly arrogant about your craft, because no one else in this Court can do what your machines do. Coro Vane of House Chains engaged you this season to surveil the heir's movements — a simple commission for a patient operator and a well-made device — though it carried your little machines somewhere you had not anticipated.
characters.post_act1_context: About a month ago you sent one of your clockwork familiars to follow Caspian as he left the estate on foot. It tracked him toward the old mill road and there, frustratingly, you lost him — the device came back with nothing. You sent it out again over the following weeks to no result, until this morning, when it returned with images of a strange site: ash, shattered glass, a scorch mark, the lingering reek of sulphur, and a fresh mound of earth resembling a mortal grave. What stopped you came earlier, though. Three weeks ago your familiars showed you that you were not the only one watching Caspian near the mill road — and where there are watchers, there are eyes that might catch the glint of brass in the dark. Your devices are worth more to you than any contract. You pulled them back rather than risk them being spotted, seized, or smashed by whoever else was out there.
secrets: About a month ago I sent one of my familiars to follow Caspian Vael as he left the estate on foot. It tracked him toward the old mill road and there I lost him — but it brought back something else. I was not the only one watching him. My devices caught others running their own surveillance: first Rynn Castille, watchful and clearly experienced, who seemed to be tracking not Caspian but a woman in his company; then a figure in a blood-red cloak, not following Caspian but already positioned ahead of him, as if anticipating his route. I do not rattle easily, but I do not risk my machines either. Where there are many trained eyes in the dark, sooner or later one catches the glint of brass, and I will not lose years of my work to finish a contract. I pulled my devices back and stopped filing reports for Coro.

After I pulled back, I waited. Then this morning, against my better judgment, I sent a single familiar out again — briefly, carefully — to the stretch of the old mill road where I had last lost Caspian, only to learn whether it was safe to risk my machines there again. The device returned with something I could never have expected: images of a strange site - ash, shattered glass, a scorch mark, the lingering reek of sulphur, and a fresh mound of earth resembling a mortal burial site.

missions.prompt: Make up a name and a backstory for a fictional clockwork familiar and tell two other players about it.
missions.answer_format: Write the name and backstory of the clockwork familiar, and the two players you told about it.

missions.prompt: Create a clockwork familiar using materials from the Reliquary, craft table, or found objects such as LEGO, pipe cleaners, paper clips, corks, or ribbon. Name it, explain its surveillance function to two players, and carry it with you for the rest of the act.
missions.answer_format: Write the familiar’s name, what it does, and the two players you demonstrated it to.

missions.prompt: Prove that at least two other people were watching Caspian, the woman in his company, or the old mill road this season. Learn who they were watching and why.
missions.answer_format: Write the two watchers, who or what they were watching, and their reason for doing so.

characters.name: Harper Hale
characters.title: The Bleeding Heart
characters.pre_event_info: Earnest, self-righteous, and relentlessly convinced you can save the Court’s soul one vial at a time. You are House Ashglass's resident black sheep — born into the Court's great house of alchemists and archivists, and quietly horrified by nearly everything it does. You do not believe blood is a substance to be synthesized, traded, drugged, or "improved." You believe it is sacred, and that it must be taken the right way: freely given, ethically sourced, from willing donors who are thanked for it — never farmed, never forced, never refined in a lab into something it was never meant to be. You bring your own tonics. You forage. You cleanse. You will, given the smallest opening, explain consent-based feeding to others at length. Your housemates find you insufferable. You find them lost. But compassion is not the same as naïvete. Beneath the granola exterior, you notice exactly who in this Court treats blood and the people it comes from as a commodity.
characters.post_act1_context: Two things have been weighing on your conscience all season. The first is the Court's communal stores: blood has been disappearing from them — small amounts, from every house, taken quietly and without anyone's leave — and to you that is not an accounting problem, it is a violation. No one consented. No one was even asked. You have been trying to make anyone care and getting nowhere. The second is heavier and more personal. Weeks before he vanished, Caspian Vael came to you — not to the alchemists — and confided that he no longer felt like himself: compelled, pulled toward things he had not chosen, as though some part of him answered to a hand that was not his own. You took it for a spiritual crisis and offered him a cleanse and a kind word. With him gone now, you wonder if he may have been trying to tell you something far more frightening than you understood.
secrets: Someone has been bleeding the Court's stores for months; small draws from all five houses, taken without requisition and without consent. To the rest of my house it is a paperwork discrepancy. To me it is a desecration: blood given by no one, taken from everyone, for a purpose no one will name. I have raised it again and again and been waved off as a crank with a cause. But blood does not simply evaporate. Someone gathered it, deliberately and in secret, and I mean to find out who, and what for.

Weeks before he vanished, Caspian Vael came to me quietly — not to the alchemists but to me. He said he no longer felt like himself: pulled toward choices he had not made, compelled by something he could not name, as if part of him answered to someone else's will. I thought he was describing a crisis of spirit. I gave him a tonic and told him to rest and reconnect with himself.

missions.prompt: Start a “humans are people too” chant and get at least two other players to join in.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the players who join in.

missions.prompt: Spread the good word. Get at least three players to share an "ethically sourced," organic tonic with you (a stand-in drink is fine) and lead them through a brief cleanse or gratitude ritual before they drink.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names and how each responded.

missions.prompt: Create a petition from “Redpeace” to the Marquess to give humans equal rights as vampires. Get at least 10 other players to sign the petition and deliver it to the Marquess.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the players who signed the petition.

characters.name: Ambrose Cipher
characters.title: He Who Peers Beyond
characters.pre_event_info: Brilliant, unraveling, and perpetually half-somewhere-else. You were the finest scholar of your generation, and it was not enough — you wanted to know the shape of everything, the machinery turning behind the world. So you found an ancient tome, etched in blood by cultists, that told of how to reach a long-forgotten god. You followed its rites and something answered. It calls itself many things; you call it your patron. It showed you the curtain pulled back: the true architecture of existence, the patterns beneath the patterns, knowledge so vast it does not fit inside a mind meant to hold ordinary things; and a mind that holds it does not stay ordinary for long. You feel the changes: conversations that arrive a half-second late, the space behind people's faces that you cannot stop staring into, the voice no one else can hear that you have begun to answer aloud. It flattered you once, made you feel the one mind worthy of its gifts, and you believed it. Then the price began to climb. A little blood at first. Then more. Then a life — and it wanted you to choose whose. Each time you feed it, it shows you something new and magnificent, the horror goes quiet, and you tell yourself the knowledge is worth what it costs. It is not. Some part of you, buried deeper each night, still knows it is not. But that part grows fainter, your patron grows louder, and tonight it is hungry.
characters.post_act1_context: Your patron has fed well this season, and a well-fed patron shows you more of the pattern than a mind can safely hold. Lately it has drawn your sight again and again to this Court, to a wrongness in it, a place where the weave of things has been pulled out of true. You do not see it as the others do, as gossip or crime. You see it as damage; a thread forced where it does not belong, a knot tied in someone that should not be tied. You have felt it worsening for weeks, and tonight, with the heir gone and the Court unraveling, the tear is wider than ever. But you can spare it only part of your attention, because the greater pressure is inside you. The debt has come due. Your patron has made plain what it wants before this night is out — not blood this time, but a life — and it has left the choosing to you. You can feel it watching through your own eyes, waiting to see whom you will bring it.
secrets: My patron speaks to me in riddles and fragments. He showed me careful hands in a place that smells of blood and alchemy, refining something over and over across this season — not a poison, not a cure, but a thing meant to reach inside another and take hold, to bend a will that was never meant to bend. I have seen him bent over it, patient as a spider, working it into blood until it would not be tasted. I do not know his name, but I know his craft: he is a physician of some kind, an alchemist, one who mends bodies by trade and has turned that skill to forging chains for the soul.

My patron showed me the truth about Valen Drear and Mara Voss during one of his lessons. Every soul gives off a kind of ripple, a frequency all its own — and theirs are wrong. Theirs move in the same rhythm, tuned to a note that is not their own, as though each has been struck by the same distant hand. I could not tell you whose hand, or how it was done. I know only that they share the same fouling and that whatever set them ringing in that borrowed key is the same wrongness spreading through this whole rotten Court.

missions.prompt: Your patron sees through you when you let it. Approach at least two players and "read" them — tell each one something the pattern reveals about who they truly are, spoken as your patron would speak it. Unsettle them.
missions.answer_format: Enter the two players you read and what you told them.

missions.prompt: Your patron takes its due. Approach at least two players and ask their leave to pour out a measure of their drink as an offering to your patron — then do it, with all the gravity such a rite deserves.
missions.answer_format: Enter the two players who granted the offering, and how each responded.
