missions.prompt: Your patron will have a life before this night is out, but it is particular: it hungers only for a thread already fraying: a soul another hand in this Court is already working to sever. Find someone who has been marked for death this night, whether they know it or not. Then go to the craft table and fashion an effigy of them — a small likeness, made with your own hands — and consecrate it to your patron before the closing, with at least two other players bearing witness to the rite. When the effigy is finished and the words are spoken, your patron will know whom you have promised it. (The rite is theater — no one comes to harm. Play it as though that were not so.)
missions.answer_format: Enter whom you chose, what marked them for death, the effigy you made, and who witnessed the rite.

characters.name: Eiran Vox
characters.title: The Spider
characters.pre_event_info:
A minor Court figure. No house allegiance. No obvious role. You have been present at every significant gathering for as long as anyone can remember, without anyone being quite sure why. You move through rooms the way water moves through stone — slowly, unremarkably, until suddenly the stone is different.

You know what Ivara Saye did. You know because knowing is what you do. You have been running the Wall of Whispers this evening. Every clue purchasable, was chosen by you. You have been seeding the right information to the right people all evening, watching the picture assemble itself.

What fewer people know: some time ago you obtained Doctor Thorne Virell's research on vampire-adapted thrall bonds — not by asking. You used it to bind Mara Voss to yourself, quietly, as a long-term investment, positioning her near succession in case the Court's arrangements ever collapsed and you needed a piece on the board. Weeks after the binding, Aurel Drear (Mara's uncle) confronted you about it. You suspect Gregor “Sour Grapes” Greer may have overheard the confrontation.

You and Gruber are operating the same mechanism (unknowingly on her part) in parallel — Thorne's work made both of you possible. At the closing ceremony, after Gruber and Valen have spoken, you will ask Ivara one question she cannot answer without confessing. You already know the answer.
characters.post_act1_context: Has attended every Crimson Toast in living memory. Holds no house allegiance. Has never competed in the Duel. Is simply, reliably, present. Collects information the way others collect artifacts — carefully, quietly, without obvious purpose. The clues available this evening were selected by him. Can occasionally be overheard asking after Mara Voss — 'Have you spoken with the Voss girl this evening?'
secrets: I have known about a specific sequence of events since the week they concluded. I chose not to intervene and not to report. I chose instead to create the conditions in which the truth could surface on its own.

I have an interest in one of the Ashglass members that goes beyond the social. I obtained what I needed to bind her to me some time ago — Thorne Virell's research, though he does not know I have it. The arrangement is stable. I find myself wanting to confirm tonight that she is still oriented correctly.

characters.name: Marquess Gruber
characters.title: The Host
characters.pre_event_info:
Ageless, theatrical, and more calculating than she will ever admit. Her reign is subject to a term limit under ancient vampire law. Rather than relinquish power, she has spent years constructing a puppet succession — binding heirs to herself through thrall rituals, then positioning one as her heir. A thrall on the throne means she rules indefinitely.

Valen's bond came first, forged through his revival after last year's Toast. She used an ancient necromantic spell she obtained from Thessaly Wren several decades earlier — designed for raising mortals, never intended for vampires. Thessaly warned her as much. The bond worked, but imperfectly — the spell was never designed for a vampire subject, and the bond began degrading almost immediately. When Valen's deterioration confirmed what Thessaly had told her, she made a decision: she would not spend herself trying to stabilize a bond that was always going to fail. Instead she went to Thorne for something new — a designed formulation, built from his deeper research, stable and undetectable. She deployed it on Caspian.

Caspian's thralldom was completely secret. Valen's visible degradation served her purposes — it drew the Court's attention to the obvious thrall and away from the hidden one. The succession announcement was never about transition. It was about continuity. She did not know Caspian was planning to break free. She did not know he would involve a mortal. She did not know about the corrupted ritual or its outcome until too late.
characters.post_act1_context: Has ruled the Crimson Court for centuries. Has never lost control of a succession. Until now. Has managed three previous succession crises — each resolved in her favor through means the Court never fully understood. Publicly omniscient. Privately blindsided by Caspian's plan.
secrets: The succession was not what it appeared to be. What I was building was not a transition — it was a continuation. I did not know what Caspian was planning. I did not know about the mortal woman. I did not know about the ritual. I have been trying to decide tonight whether my ignorance is a defense or an indictment.
I chose to let Valen's bond degrade. Maintaining two vampire-thralls at full strength is not sustainable. Caspian's bond was cleaner, quieter, and gave me the continuation I needed.

characters.name: Fenric Solle
characters.title: The Kitchen Steward
characters.pre_event_info: Thirty years of service has taught you one thing above all: the most dangerous conversations happen when people forget you are in the room. You run the Marquess's kitchens, overseeing every meal, every provision, every ceremonial vessel, and every servant who moves through the household's back passages. Nothing enters or leaves this household without passing, one way or another, through you. The kitchen sees everything: who comes and goes through the servants' corridors, what gets carried in at odd hours, and what leaves the estate that shouldn't. You are not political — you leave the scheming to your betters and outlast them all regardless. You simply notice things, the way you notice a sauce about to break or a guest who has had one glass too many. And you do love a bit of gossip; three centuries of it, traded over the chopping block, and you have found there is no better currency in this Court than knowing something juicy before it goes cold.
characters.post_act1_context: You have observations from this season that you have not shared with anyone. First: Doctor Thorne Virell visited the Marquess's private quarters on at least four occasions in the past six months, always through the servants' passage. On his last visit, you noticed he was carrying a small sealed case on arrival and left without it. Second: two ceremonial vessels were requested outside the normal occasions this season — one by Caspian Vael several weeks ago, one set aside privately just this week. Both struck you as irregular. You prepared them as asked and said nothing. You have learned, over thirty years, that a steward who repeats what he sees does not stay a steward for long.
secrets: The kitchen issues and stores the household's ceremonial vessels, so I notice when one is requested outside the normal occasions. Twice this season that has happened. Several weeks ago Caspian Vael asked me privately for a ceremonial glass vessel — the old kind, used in the oldest rites — and took it himself rather than letting it be delivered. Then this week, someone requested the golden ceremonial chalice be prepared and set aside outside the service rotation, for a private presentation rather than the ceremony. I prepared both as asked and noted who collected them. Two irregular requests in one season is one more than I am comfortable with. I have said nothing.

Doctor Thorne Virell visited the Marquess's private quarters at least four times in the past six months, always through the servants' passage and never announced. On his last visit, I noticed he was carrying a small sealed case on arrival and left without it. I do not know what the case contained.

Disposal of the household's waste runs through my kitchens — what leaves the estate, leaves through me. After last year's ceremony a sealed order came through for a hazardous vial to be collected from Dr. Thorne’s lab and destroyed by the biohazard ghouls. I logged it as handled. But the collectors later told me nothing of that description ever reached them. I have wondered since whether something went missing on my watch.

missions.prompt: Prepare and serve a snack to at least two different players.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the players and the snacks you served them.

missions.prompt: Offer to make a drink for one player in exchange for one of their secrets and have them accept.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of the players who accepted your trade and the drinks you served them.

missions.prompt: Find out who intercepted the hazardous vial and prevented it from being disposed of by the biohazard ghouls.
missions.answer_format: Write the identity of the thief.

characters.name: Magistra Moore
characters.title: The Duel Judge
characters.pre_event_info: Icy, ancient, and loyal to law above all else. You hold the most senior legal appointment in the Crimson Court — named by the Sanguine Statutes themselves as its permanent succession adjudicator, a post no Marquess can grant and none can revoke. You have presided over every succession crisis in living vampiric memory, and you have watched what this Court becomes when the law slips even for a moment. Vampires are the oldest predators in the world, and the Statutes are the single thread that keeps them from tearing one another, and everything else, apart. You believe in that thread the way others believe in blood or power. It has not made you beloved. A judge who cannot be flattered, bribed, or moved by sentiment is respected, feared, and kept at arm's length, and you made your peace with that solitude centuries ago. You do not attend the Toast to celebrate. You attend to watch; because succession is when the law is tested hardest, and when the ambitious mistake a ceremony for an opening. There is almost nothing you would not do to see the Statutes upheld. That certainty is your greatest strength and, at times, your greatest weakness.
characters.post_act1_context: You are the foremost living authority on the Sanguine Statutes and the Crimson Codex, and tonight, with the succession in open chaos, players may seek you out for rulings and interpretations — a role you take with deadly seriousness. But your attention is elsewhere. A few months before tonight's ceremony, Valen Drear came to you and could not force out whatever he had come to say, and that wordless struggle told you more than any confession could. You have been building a case ever since; watching Valen, observing the Marquess, cross-referencing what you see against centuries of precedent held in your memory. As part of that work you paid the junior diplomat Pax Delenne for anything unusual she had witnessed, and what she described has not left you: a tall, scarred man trading a purse for a bound document from Silas Venn. You do not yet know whether it touches your case. But tonight the pieces are moving faster than the law likes to move and you are increasingly aware that you are not the only one who wants the Marquess answered for, nor the only one prepared to act.
secrets: I have prosecuted thrall cases before, and I know the signs. A few months ago Valen Drear approached me but could not say what he wanted to; something was physically stopping him from forming the words. After that interaction I began to strongly suspect Valen is bound, likely to the Marquess. She is the one who revived him, and the only person I believe has the standing and power to create a thrall bond. I began building a Sanguine Statutes case immediately. If I am right, the charge is severe: unlawful thralldom, compounded by concealment, merits not just removal from the throne but banishment from the Court entirely.

As part of my investigations, I asked junior diplomat Pax Delenne if she had seen anything suspicious recently. She shared information in exchange for a fee: several weeks ago, she witnessed a tall, scarred man conversing with Silas Venn. Silas passed the man a bound document, and the man handed him a purse in exchange. I haven’t yet determined if it connects to the case I’m building.

There are others in this Court who want the Marquess gone by any means; Aldric spoke to me of it months ago, but I refused to learn more. If she is to fall, it will be by the Sanguine Statutes and the weight of evidence, not by a knife in the dark. I will not become what I am trying to remove, but am no longer certain the others will wait for the law.

missions.prompt: Tell two different players that you will not tolerate any funny business during this year’s toast and that “you have your eye on them.”
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the characters you warned.

missions.prompt: Formally adjudicate one dispute or argument between players during the evening.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of both parties and your ruling.

missions.prompt: Gather testimony from 3 different players to support your theory about Valen’s illegal thrall bond and present it to Marquess Gruber. You lose this mission if any of the vigilantes who want the Marquess gone find the means to accomplish their goal before you do so.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the 3 players and what information they shared to support Valen’s bond.

characters.name: Kestrel Solle
characters.title: The Secretary Spy
characters.pre_event_info: You hold the official role of secretary in the Marquess's household — managing schedules, correspondence, and the daily logistics of the Court's inner circle. It is quiet, invisible work, and such invisibility suits you. You have made a long career of being the person in the room who no one thinks to stop talking in front of. While your loyalties lie with the Court, you are also a paid informant for House Chains, and you have learned to hold those two allegiances carefully. You are precise, unobtrusive, and excellent at both jobs. Above all, you have been doing this long enough to know the most important skill in your trade: not just gathering information, but judging when a piece of it is too dangerous to pass in the wrong direction. Most of what you learn, you sell. The rest you bury, because a spy who knows the difference is a spy who survives.
characters.post_act1_context: Several weeks ago you were paid by Madam Anbarin to intercept Caspian's incoming correspondence — routine work, the kind you have done a hundred times without a second thought. Then one letter stopped you cold: Caspian's reply to Lysa Vael, asking her to meet him privately and promising to reveal everything. That his succession was not what it appeared to be. You have handled dangerous information before and always known exactly where to route it. This time you did not. You did not pass it to Anbarin. You gave it to the Marquess instead, fearing what would happen to your position if something this large unraveled and you were found to have been holding the thread. Coro Vane, the House of Chains debt collector, has been pressing you for repayment ever since. Chains does not forget an informant who goes quiet at the wrong moment. And tonight, with Caspian gone and the Court coming apart, you have begun to wonder whether the cautious move and the safe move were the same thing after all.
secrets: Several weeks ago I intercepted a letter from Caspian to Lysa Vael. He was responding to her concern for his wellbeing — and what he wrote back was not reassurance. He asked her to meet him, named a place, and said he would reveal everything: that his succession was not what it appeared to be. I did not pass it to Madam Anbarin as instructed. I gave it to the Marquess instead. I told myself it was the cautious move. I have been less certain of that with every hour that has passed tonight.

Five months ago I saw the Marquess descend to her private wine cellar alone and emerge carrying one of her finer vintages — not house stock, something personal. She went directly to Caspian's quarters and closed the door behind them. No staff. No ceremony. I was the only one in that corridor. I have told no one.

Earlier this year, Brother Aldric drew me into a small circle who believed the Marquess had held power too long and meant the Court harm. We met in secret, masked, never showing our faces to one another; only Aldric knew who we all were. Now, with Aldric missing, we are fragmented and uncertain, and I do not even know for certain who else stood beside me in that garden. I trust no one tonight.

missions.prompt: Eavesdrop on a conversation between two players from the same house and report its contents to a Chains member before Act 2 ends.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of the two players you overheard and the key thing they said.

missions.prompt: Pass a piece of Court intelligence to your Chains contact without anyone in the Court noticing the exchange. On confirmation, Marquess's Court loses 3 HF.
missions.answer_format: Write what you passed and the character name of who received it.

missions.prompt: Identify two other members of the conspiracy (besides yourself and Brother Aldric) to take down the Marquess. You fail this mission if Petra Nox finds out that you are part of the resistance.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the two characters you identified.

characters.name: Navine Solera
characters.title: The Excommunicated Seer
characters.pre_event_info: Before your banishment, you served as Ashglass's resident seer - formally appointed to advise the house on matters of prophecy and foresight. Though your visions were often frustratingly cryptic, they had earned you a reputation for uncanny accuracy and a certain smug confidence that many found difficult to tolerate. Your appointment ended when you delivered a prediction your house branded incorrect, providing rivals with the excuse they needed to push you aside. The Marquess, however, recognized both your talent and your value, and was happy to add you to her court. You remain observant, enigmatic, and perhaps a bit too fond of reminding people that prophecies are rarely understood when they are first spoken. You have made peace with being disbelieved; you have even come to enjoy it, in a way, because being disbelieved and then proven right is a particular pleasure the patient can savor and the foolish never taste. You said what you saw, and they will remember that you said it. They always do, eventually — usually too late.
characters.post_act1_context: Before your banishment you foresaw this: a hand on two chains, one loose and rusted, the other pulled taut — meaning neither heir had been free for some time. You told Eiran Vox. He said: "I know. It will resolve itself." He did not explain. Tonight you have been watching him work the room and wondering what "resolve itself" might look like — and feeling the particular dread of a seer who has seen a thing begin to come true and can do nothing but watch it arrive. You spent years being told your visions were riddles and errors. Tonight the riddles are unfolding into fact around you, one by one. You have always taken a quiet pleasure in being proven right. This is the first time it has brought you none.
secrets: Several weeks before tonight's ceremony I told Eiran Vox what I had foreseen - a hand on two chains, one loose and rusted, the other pulled taut. Neither heir was free, I told him; they have not been free for some time. He listened without surprise. When I finished he said only: 'I know. It will resolve itself.' He did not explain what he meant. I have been watching him all evening, waiting to see what 'resolve itself' looks like.

My banishment was not the misjudgment the Court believes. I foresaw what the Marquess would become and said so too plainly. Lysa Vael saw my prophecy as bad for business and had me banished from the house before Marquess Gruber could get word of it.

Not long before he vanished, Brother Aldric came to me. He believed what I had foreseen, and he was building something to act on it: a small circle, meeting masked so none of us knew the others, who agreed the Marquess had to be removed. I joined them. Then Aldric disappeared, and the rest of us are left masked from one another, unsure whether to go forward or pretend it never began.

missions.prompt: Engage two different players in conversation and tell them made up prophecies about themselves.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of both players and the prophecies you made up about them.

missions.prompt: Tell two players of your prophecy from your first secret and attempt to convince them of a specific interpretation of it. These players must be from two different houses not of your own.
missions.answer_format: Write each player's character name and what you told them your prophecy meant.

missions.prompt: You have received a new vision: two merchants standing beneath a black sun. One holds a golden thorn tipped with blood. The other raises a goblet weeping black poison. Between them lies the Marquess, crumbling to dust, untouched by ordinary blades. Interpret the vision. Identify the two merchants and their significance, then pass this knowledge to two other members of Aldric’s resistance. This mission fails if you cannot identify both merchants, or if you fail to deliver the information to two resistance members before the plot against the Marquess moves forward.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the two merchants, their significance, and the two resistance members you informed.

characters.name: Orla Thass
characters.title: The Masked Sommelier
characters.pre_event_info: Bound by oath and masked by ritual, you have served as the Crimson Toast's officiating sommelier for over a century. You select, pour, and present the wine at every formal ceremony — and in the Court, wine does not become ceremonial until it passes through your hands. A glass from you can seal a fate: an alliance, pardon, succession, or even exile. You notice what others miss: whose hands tremble, whose glass stays untouched, whose toast is swallowed too quickly, and whose refusal says more than any speech. You serve the Marquess, but your true loyalty is older than any individual ruler. It lies with the Crimson Toast itself and all of its rituals. You have poured for seven successions and outlasted three of the rulers you crowned with a glass; behind the mask, you have become less a person than an instrument of the rite, and you prefer it that way: a person can be bribed, frightened, or moved to pity, but an instrument simply performs its function. Rulers die. Heirs vanish. Houses rot. The ritual remains. Or it should.
characters.post_act1_context: Last month, you observed Caspian decline wine at a private Court function. For the heir to a wine dynasty, during a ceremonial tasting, this was extraordinary. He did not look rebellious. He did not look ill. He looked afraid. You noted it and said nothing. It is not your place to comment on what guests drink. Only to pour. This morning, you found an unsigned note slipped beneath your door. It said only: “Do not pour the wine tonight.” No signature. No house seal. A hand you did not recognize, the message anonymous, deliberate, and unadorned. Any other servant might heed it. But you have poured through plague, through coups, through the poisoning of a Marquess two centuries dead, and you have never once let fear stay your hand. The rite does not bend to anonymous notes. You will pour tonight, as you have always poured, warnings be damned.
secrets: Last month, Caspian declined wine at a private ceremonial tasting. For an heir to a wine dynasty this was unthinkable. I said nothing and poured anyway.

I am aware that Gregor “Sour Grapes” Greer of House Spires believes Valen Drear cheated to beat him in the competition to become the House Spires taster. I know for a fact that he is mistaken. I poured the wine myself. There was no foul play.

This morning I found an unsigned note slipped under my door. It said only: "Do not pour the wine tonight." No signature, no house seal, and a hand I did not recognize — the words plain and deliberate, written by someone who seemed to know exactly what they were asking for.

missions.prompt: Pour a glass of wine for two different players and recite a brief ceremonial blessing as each receives it.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the players who received the glasses of wine and the blessings you gave them.

missions.prompt: Ask three players whether they have ever seen Caspian refuse wine, hesitate before drinking, or act strangely during a toast.
missions.answer_format: Write the three character names and what each one told you.

missions.prompt: Orchestrate a formal toast involving at least four players from at least three different houses. You initiate, pour, and deliver a ceremonial line.
missions.answer_format: Write the character names of all four players who participated.

characters.name: Petra Nox
characters.title: The Bodyguard
characters.pre_event_info: Silent, watchful, and never more than a few steps from the Marquess when it matters. Your official role is personal bodyguard to Marquess Gruber: you control who gets close to her, manage her movements through the estate, and stand between her and anything that might threaten her. You have held this post for longer than most of the Court has been alive. You know her routines, her habits, and her private spaces better than anyone. You are not politically ambitious. Your loyalty is to her person, not to her politics — a distinction you have learned to keep to yourself. You have guarded her through six succession crises and more attempts on her life than the Court will ever know, and in all that time you have never once asked what she does with the power you keep her alive to hold. It is not your business. Your business is the space around her: who enters it, who lingers in it, who reaches for her with something other than a handshake. Let the houses scheme over what kind of ruler she is. You have only ever needed to know that she is yours to protect.
characters.post_act1_context: You have stood close to the Marquess all season and noticed things others have not. Earlier this year she ordered you to admit Doctor Thorne Virell through the servants' route and then to leave them entirely alone — something she has never asked of you for anyone. And you have long known of the single bottle of wine she keeps apart from the cellar, in her private study, handled only by her. You guard her body. You have learned, over the years, to guard her secrets too — even the ones she does not know you have noticed. And this season there have been more of them than ever: the doctor she would not let you watch, the bottle she trusts to no hand but her own, the heir who is suddenly gone. You do not know how the pieces fit. You only know that the woman you have protected for centuries is afraid of something, and a Marquess who is afraid is a Marquess in danger; which makes it, whether you like it or not, your business at last.
secrets: The Marquess keeps one bottle of wine entirely separate from the general cellar — stored in her private study, handled only by her, never left unattended. As her bodyguard I am close enough to know every habit she has, and this one began shortly after Valen's revival. I have never been asked about it and I have never volunteered what I know.

Part of my duty is controlling who reaches the Marquess in private. Earlier this season she ordered me to admit Doctor Thorne Virell through the servants' route and then to leave them entirely alone — no guard, no staff, which she has never asked of me for anyone. Whatever passed between them, she did not want it witnessed. I obeyed. I have wondered ever since what was worth that much secrecy.

Late last night, with the ceremony looming, I intercepted Brother Aldric Voss and had him confined. I care nothing for the politics of it, but this was no debate over policy. Aldric was at the heart of a circle that wanted the Marquess dead, and I had watched him long enough to know he meant to move tonight. He had come back to the estate to rally the others with something he claimed to have witnessed, and I took him before he reached them. I am keeping him alive and whole because he is the only one who knows who else stood with him, and I mean to have every last one before I am finished. Some of them are in this room tonight. I can feel it.

characters.name: Rynn 'The Hollow' Castille
characters.title: The Hunter Turned Hunted
characters.pre_event_info: You are what remains of The Hollow Sash: a vampire hunting order dedicated to protecting mortals who become entangled with Court politics. Last year's ceremony ended badly for you. You were identified as a hunter, and the Court made an example of you. You were turned. You did not ask for it and have not forgiven it. Every night since, you have worn the body of your enemy, felt its hungers, and hated yourself a little more for how easily they come. You hold fast to everything you believed before — the mission, the order, the cause — and participate in vampyrdom only as much as survival requires. Inwardly you are disgusted with what you have become. You are still here only because Lena, a mortal woman you had been watching for her own protection, has been seen visiting with members of this vampire Court. You have been tracking her movements for some time.
characters.post_act1_context: Last night, you were following Lena at a distance; she did not know you were there. You saw her approach the mill house and go inside alone. Then you heard it: a sound like an implosion, a flash of light through the cracks in the walls. When you reached the site you found her body — intact, but bled dry — amid the remains of whatever ritual had taken place inside. You removed her and buried her as best you could. You have told no one what you saw, but you do not intend to sit idly. You do not know who was individually responsible, and frankly you do not care. The Court turned you against your will; the Court is responsible for Lena's death. The Marquess sits at the head of that snake, and tonight, you mean to end her.
secrets: Last night, I was following Lena at a distance; she did not know I was there. I saw her approach the mill house and go inside alone. Then I heard a sound like an implosion and saw a flash of light. When I reached the site I found her body, intact but bled dry. I removed her body and gave my best attempt at a proper burial outside. I have been telling myself I couldn't have known.

Lena was not someone who stumbled into the Court's orbit by accident. She had been watching this world for some time before she chose to enter it. She understood what these people were. She made her own decisions about the risks. She told me once that she believed mortals who chose to engage with vampires deserved protection, not rescue, and that the difference mattered. I disagreed with her about that, and still do. But her death was not ignorance. I believe someone in this room made a choice that killed her.

Brother Aldric drew me into a masked circle of Court figures moving against the Marquess; like the others, I never knew their faces and they never knew mine. I do not share their politics or their patience. I let Aldric believe I joined for the cause. I did not. The Court turned me against my will, and now a woman I swore to protect is dead in the orbit of this Court. What I intend is simpler than a coup. If the chance comes tonight to end the Marquess, I will take it.

missions.prompt: Tell two characters what you know about Lena.
missions.answer_format: Write their character names.

missions.prompt: A member of the Court who is in this room tonight was pursuing Lena’s affections a few weeks before tonight’s ceremony. His love for her was unrequited and you believe he may still be holding a grudge. Run an investigation to determine which player this was.
missions.answer_format: Write the character name of the player you believe was pursuing Lena (note: must be a player who is present in the Court tonight).

missions.prompt: Obtain the means to end the Marquess and use it. You lose this mission if either Petra Nox or Magistra Moore accuse you as a conspirator. Rumor has it that a seers recent prophecy may help to guide your hand.
missions.answer_format: Write the name of the implement you used to do the deed.

characters.name: Silas Venn
characters.title: The Antiquarian Broker
characters.pre_event_info: Charm with a price tag, and a memory that never closes an account. While you hold no official post in the Crimson Court, you are known by most as a relic hunter, archivist of shadows, and auctioneer of secrets. Titles bind you to a single house; you prefer to be useful to all of them and beholden to none. You return to the Crimson Toast each year because it is the richest market in the vampiric world for the kind of things you deal in: pre-Sanguine Statutes artifacts, restricted documents, and intelligence that no one else has thought to monetize. You are cunning, attentive, and genuinely good company, which is precisely what makes you dangerous. People trust you to handle their most unscrupulous requests with subtlety and discretion. You sold a forged relic to House Spires years ago and have been waiting, with a gambler's calm, to see whether that particular debt ever comes due.
characters.post_act1_context: Several months ago a buyer wrote to you seeking intelligence on an old ritual, the Rite of Undoing. The letter was anonymous, but it bore the Marquess's seal, so you asked no questions and the payment was generous besides. When you met to exchange payment for what you'd gathered, the buyer came hooded and masked; he was tall, and the only thing you could see of him were his eyes: a striking, bright blue, with a scar drawn across the left one. You never learned his name. You paid Jasper Delle to locate the text in the Ashglass archives and bring back what he safely could. Jasper returned with the location and the ritual's requirements — ingredients, conditions, and participants — though not the full procedure. A few weeks later Ivara Saye came to you, citing the Sanguine Statutes and making clear she could have you sanctioned. You told her what you knew: that you had been paid anonymously to source the intelligence, and that Jasper had found the text. You could not give her a name, but passed along what you remembered from your exchange. You have been trying to work out who your buyer might have been ever since.
secrets: Several months ago a letter reached me, anonymous but sealed with the Marquess's own seal, seeking intelligence on an old ritual, the Rite of Undoing. The seal was reason enough to ask no questions, and the payment promised was generous besides. When we met to exchange payment for what I'd gathered, the buyer came hooded and masked; he was tall, and the only thing I could see of him were his eyes: a striking, bright blue, with an old scar drawn across the left one. I paid Jasper Delle to locate the text in the Ashglass archives and bring back what he safely could; he returned with the location and the ritual's requirements — ingredients, conditions, and participants — though not the full procedure. I passed it all to the masked man, though I never learned his name.

A few weeks later Ivara Saye came to me, citing the Sanguine Statutes and making clear she had the authority to have me sanctioned. I told her the truth as I knew it: that I had been paid anonymously to source intelligence on the Rite of Undoing, that Jasper Delle had located the text, and I described what little I saw of the buyer. I could not give her a name, but she seemed to already suspect who it was. She thanked me and left without sharing anything more.

I possess a golden stake capable of killing a powerful vampire. I have not yet decided whether it should be used, hidden, or sold. My loyalty belongs to whichever side makes the strongest offer. I have also heard through the grapevine that a few court members have need of such an object tonight.

characters.name: Uma Brynmore
characters.title: The Werewolf Ambassador
characters.pre_event_info: Watchful, proud, and perpetually half-welcome. You are the ambassador of the werewolf clans to the Crimson Court — the lone envoy of a people the vampires tolerate by treaty and trust by habit, which is to say not at all. You attend the Toast because the old accords require a witness from the clans, and because vampires who believe they are the only predators in the room grow careless about what they say in front of you. You are not one of them and never will be. Where they see only with their eyes, you know a person by scent: health, fear, lineage, what they last ate, what they bled, what has been done to them. Blood does not lie to a nose like yours. Vampires would do well to realize that.
characters.post_act1_context: Your nose has been troubling you all season, catching what the rest of this Court cannot. More than once, someone has brushed past you carrying a scent that did not belong to them: blood that wasn't wholly their own. Each time, before you could fix on it, they were gone into the crowd, faces unseen. Two nights ago the wind off the outer road brought something worse. Scorched blood, sulphur, ash; the reek of a ritual gone violently wrong. It was there by dark and gone by morning, and no vampire here seems to have noticed but you. You have said nothing. You simply added it to the ledger you have been keeping all season: a ledger of every weakness this Court has unknowingly shown you. When you return to the clans, your account of such weaknesses will decide what comes next.
secrets: Vampires trust their eyes, but forget others still hunt by nose. At least three times this season someone has passed close to me carrying a scent that does not belong to them — another vampire's blood layered beneath their own, or the sharp trace of something alchemical worked into it. Each time I marked the smell and missed the face, and by the time I turned they were gone into the crowd. I cannot tell you who they were, but believe there are at least three of them in this Court tonight, all carrying blood that is not wholly theirs.

Two nights ago the wind off the outer road carried something I have not smelled in a long time: scorched blood, and beneath it sulphur and ash — the particular reek of a ritual that has gone violently wrong. It was gone by morning and no vampire here seems to have noticed. But the clans know that smell. Something burned out near the old mill road, and whatever it was, it was not an ordinary fire.

I am here under the old accords, but the accords are thinner than the vampires think. The clans did not send me merely to witness the Toast. They sent me to spy: to measure this Court’s strength, identify its fractures, and learn which vampires are dangerous, which are vulnerable, and which could be turned against one another. What I report to the clans may decide whether we honor the treaty for another generation — or strike while the Court is weakest.

missions.prompt: Sniff two players and tell them that it's the traditional werewolf greeting.
missions.answer_format: Write the names of the players you sniffed.

missions.prompt: Compile a dossier for the werewolf clans assessing the Crimson Court’s weaknesses. Identify one vampire who is truly dangerous, one who is politically vulnerable, and one major fracture in the Court that the clans could exploit.
missions.answer_format: Write the dangerous vampire, the vulnerable vampire, and the weakness or division you would report to the clans.

missions.prompt: Identify one plot or opportunity tonight that could materially weaken the Crimson Court. You may complete this mission in one of two ways: Option A: Identify an assassination plot and provide meaningful aid to help it succeed. Your aid may include a relic, a dangerous secret related to the mission, an introduction to a necessary accomplice, or a distraction. Option B: Personally secure a crown that grants a “concrete strategic advantage” over the Court for the werewolf clans. The item that provides a “concrete strategic advantage over the court” will explicitly say so.
missions.answer_format: Write the plot or advantage you identified, what you secured or provided, and how it could weaken the Court.

characters.name: Rowan Mauve
characters.title: The Kingmaker
characters.pre_event_info: Polished, patient, and always three favors ahead, you are a career politician of House Spires. You have spent decades climbing the Court's councils, brokering votes, trading permissions, and authoring resolutions that praise whoever happens to be winning. You have never held the top of anything, and you have never fallen from anything either. Your rival on that climb, for longer than either of you will admit, is Aurel Voss. Both of you hungry, both of you Spires, both of you reaching for the same rungs. He climbs loudly. You climb quietly, and you keep a ledger of everything you learn on the way up. Several months ago, Brother Aldric Voss asked you privately whether you believed the Court would survive another century under the Marquess. You gave him a smile and a question in return. Then he invited you to a meeting, and you went, because you have never once declined a room where power might change hands. You attended the masked circle the way you attend everything: listening, cataloguing, committing to nothing. If Gruber falls, you were there early. If the circle falls, no one knows your face. You do not consider yourself a revolutionary. Revolutions are for people without better options. You are simply a woman who likes to hold tickets to every outcome.
characters.post_act1_context: Aldric's disappearance has turned your hedge into a live wire. He was the only member of the masked circle whose identity everyone knew; the rest of you met anonymously, faces hidden, names withheld. Days before he vanished, he told you he had found evidence that someone had altered an ancient text in the Ashglass archives, and he seemed frightened rather than triumphant. Now Caspian is missing, and Aldric is gone, and Mara Voss sits third in the line of succession. Which means the other item in your ledger, the one concerning Mara, has appreciated considerably in value. Tonight you are doing what you always do: watching the board, and deciding when to spend what you hold.
secrets: I attended several of Brother Aldric's masked meetings. We never revealed our identities to one another; Aldric was the only name any of us knew. Three days before the ceremony, he told me he had discovered evidence that someone had tampered with an ancient text. He was not triumphant. He was frightened. He told me, "If I'm right, then someone is about to die." Then he vanished. If my presence in that circle ever surfaces, my career ends with it.

I keep an informant in the Ashglass infirmary, as any sensible politician does. Weeks ago she told me that Mara Voss, Aurel's niece, has changed. Quiet, distracted, deferential in the way of someone waiting for an instruction. I believe the girl is bound to someone, and I believe Aurel knows. I have spent decades losing rungs to that man, and this is the kind of leverage that ends a rivalry. I have not yet decided when to spend it.

missions.prompt: A politician is nothing without visible supporters. Adorn a temporary tattoo as the mark of your political faction, then convince three other members of the Court to wear one as well, in a show of support and alliance. Note: the tattoos do not need to match, everyone just needs to wear one.
missions.answer_format: Enter the names of the three characters sporting a tattoo as a symbol of allegiance.

missions.prompt: Find Mara Voss and speak with her privately before Act 2 ends. Then talk to other players or purchase clues to assemble a theory about what is happening with Mara. Present your theory to at least two players from different houses.
missions.answer_format: Write both character names and the theory you presented.

missions.prompt: Decide whether Aldric truly uncovered something dangerous. To complete this mission, identify the altered ritual or suspicious record and explain how it connects to someone's death, disappearance, or planned death.
missions.answer_format: Enter your conclusion, the evidence, and who you warned.

characters.name: Belladonna Motley
characters.title: The Licensed Fool
characters.pre_event_info:
Bright-eyed, sharp-tongued, and far less harmless than your bells suggest. You are Belladonna Motley, the Court's licensed fool: the only person in the Crimson Court permitted to mock power to its face and live.

Every house has traditions. The Court has you.

You perform at ceremonies, interrupt speeches, sing filthy little verses about ancient bloodlines, and say aloud what everyone else is too frightened or too polite to mention. The nobles laugh because they are expected to. The Marquess laughs because it reminds everyone that she is powerful enough to be mocked without being threatened.

That is the official arrangement, anyway.

The truth is more useful. A joke can enter rooms where accusations cannot. A rhyme can test a guilty conscience. A cruel little song can make a liar flinch before they remember they are supposed to smile.

You are not a prophet, not a spy, and certainly not a statesman. You are something older and more dangerous: a sanctioned disruption. The Court has given you permission to tell the truth, provided everyone can pretend you did not mean it.

You mean almost all of it.

And despite what people assume, mockery is not hatred. You have spent too many years watching the Court from the edge of every room to believe that tearing down power is the same thing as improving it. The Marquess is vain, ruthless, controlling, and far too pleased with herself.

She is also the reason this room has not eaten itself alive.

You are not blind to her sins. You are simply old enough to know that conspirators are rarely as noble as they sound before the knives come out.
characters.post_act1_context:
Tonight, the jokes are landing strangely.

Before the ceremony, you did what you always do: wandered the room, needled the proud, flattered the foolish, and watched who laughed too quickly. But Valen Drear has been wrong all evening. You made a passing joke about heirs on strings and masters with bloody hands. Valen laughed once, then went still, as though the laugh had been pulled out of him and then punished.

The Marquess noticed too.

That is when you knew the joke had found a wound.

Now Caspian has failed to appear, Valen has stepped forward, and the Court is pretending this is still a ceremony instead of a room full of knives. Everyone is suddenly speaking carefully. Everyone is suddenly listening.

You can feel the shape of conspiracy in the room: the little silences, the sudden glances, the people who look angry before they look afraid. You do not know who means to move against the Marquess tonight, but you are increasingly certain someone does.

Perhaps they are right about her.

Perhaps they are also fools.

If the Marquess falls tonight, it will not be into a neat legal succession and a polite round of applause. It will be blood, panic, opportunists, and every ambitious creature in the Court discovering that “liberation” is just another word for an empty chair.

Which means tonight, your license may be more useful than ever.

You can say what others cannot. You can mock treason before it becomes fashionable. You can make rebellion sound ridiculous, disloyal, premature, or dangerous. You can defend the Marquess in the only way the Court will accept from you: as a joke everyone slowly realizes was serious.
secrets: Earlier tonight, I made a joke about heirs on strings and masters with bloody hands. Valen Drear laughed once, then went still in a way I did not like at all. The Marquess saw it happen. Her face did not change much, but I have spent years making powerful people laugh. I know the difference between amusement and damage control.

I mock the Marquess more than anyone in this Court, but I do not want her torn down by fools with daggers and slogans. A bad ruler is dangerous. A power vacuum is worse. If Gruber falls tonight without law, order, or a clear successor, the Court will not become free. It will become meat.

My license protects me only so long as the Court finds me useful. I can mock the Marquess, insult the houses, and say dangerous things in public, but the privilege has limits. If I press too hard tonight — against the Marquess or against those plotting against her — someone may decide the fool has stopped being funny.

missions.prompt: Publicly insult, tease, or mock three players from three different houses. Each insult must be delivered in character as a joke, rhyme, toast, title, or “official fool's observation.”
missions.answer_format: Write the three character names and the insult you gave each one.

missions.prompt: The Fool's Rumor Mill. Start one ridiculous but plausible rumor about tonight's events. Later, find out whether it returned to you changed, improved, distorted, or accidentally closer to the truth. The rumor may concern Caspian's absence, Valen's behavior, the Marquess, secret meetings, succession chaos, blood moving where it should not, or another danger tonight.
missions.answer_format: Write the rumor you started, who you told first, and how it changed when you heard it again.

missions.prompt: Propaganda in Bells. Create and spread a pro-Marquess slogan, toast, rhyme, or talking point. It must defend her authority, undermine the conspirators, or make rebellion seem foolish, dangerous, premature, or likely to plunge the Court into chaos. Get at least five players to hear it, and at least two players to repeat it, agree with it, or use it in conversation with someone else.
missions.answer_format: Write the propaganda you created, the five players who heard it, and the two who repeated, agreed with, or supported it.
